Åbo Akademi University
Description
Åbo Akademi University is Finland’s only Swedish-language multidisciplinary university, founded in 1918, with its main activities in two campuses: Åbo (Turku) and Vasa. It has around 7,000 students, including about 1,000 international students each year. The official language of instruction is Swedish, but there is a wide array of courses taught in English (around 250 annually) for exchange students. 
Turku (Åbo in Swedish) is Finland’s oldest city and a charming place to live and study. With over 40,000 students in the city and a historic, cultural atmosphere, there's always something going on — theatre, museums or festivals. Turku lies on the southwest coast of Finland, close to the archipelago, has good transport links to other parts of Finland and beyond, and provides a picturesque natural environment (coastline, islands, parks) for both study and recreation.

Arizona State University
Description
The university was founded in 1885 and is among the largest universities in the US with more than 74,000 students from 120 countries. ASU has four campuses located throughout the Phoenix metropolitan area, but are easily accessible by free intercampus shuttles. While international exchange students can study at any campus, most take courses at the Tempe campus due to the high number of classes and programs offered. Tempe is a lively university city, only a five-minute drive from Phoenix.

Above 7 (Danish scale) - please note that when applying for graduate level in the US you have to fulfill the grade requirement from the department at your host university. Each department has their own grade requirement for graduate level students - and it is often very high -  often between 10 and 12.

IELTS-test: 6.0
Study load for undergraduate students

Study load for graduate students
9 units = 30 ECTS

Restrictions
Course Restrictions:
W.P. Carey School of Business (undergraduate programs): seats are limited depending on the specific academic unit or course; one example is Supply Chain Management (SCM) which has extremely limited spaces for exchange students.
 W.P. Carey School of Business (MBA): prior approval required during exchange program nomination phase (limited course offerings)
 Herberger Institute for Design and the Arts: students studying a similar major at their home university will be required to submit a portfolio of work or audition to gain access to courses
 Ira A. Fulton Schools of Engineering: Engineering students cannot take any engineering capstone courses at ASU.  In addition, some schools in engineering have further enrollment restrictions for engineering students:
 Schools of Computing and Augmented Intelligence: Students can take any lower division courses they wish.  For upper division courses, they need instructor approval to receive access to the course.
 Sustainable Engineering and the Built Environment: If there are a large number of open seats, students will be allowed to enroll in courses for which they meet prerequisites.  If there are very few seats available, advisors will wait until 1-2 weeks before the start of the semester before giving permission for exchange students to enroll.
 Engineering of Matter, Transport, and Energy: After all registration has opened, students may be given overrides into courses that still have a lot of seats open, depending on the course demand for ASU majors.  For these courses, advisers will wait until the FIRST WEEK of the semester before giving permission for exchange students to enroll.
 Walter Cronkite School of Journalism & Mass Comm: JMC courses are not available; MCO courses are limited.  Alternative courses in other colleges may be found under the following prefixes: COM & ENG
 College of Health Solutions: seats are limited depending on the specific academic unit or course
 Edson College of Nursing and Health Innovation: most courses are restricted
 Mary Lou Fulton Teacher’s College: most courses are restricted
Course information
Students should use the most recent semester Class Search (LINK) to search course offerings, as the Course Catalog Search will include many courses that are not currently scheduled.
  International exchange students are eligible to enroll in most classes for which they meet applicable prerequisites, as long as the course has not filled to capacity.   Please note that students are not guaranteed to receive all of their preferred classes, even if they meet prerequisites. 
Online courses: ASU offers several iCourses that are 100% online; international exchange students may take iCourses, but must be enrolled in at least 9 credits of in-person coursework (undergraduate) or 6 credits of in-person coursework (graduate).
  Internet - Hybrid courses: ASU offers several courses that have both an inperson and online component. International exchange students are not restricted from taking hybrid classes. Course Restrict
Important links
ASU International Exchange Program website

Housing
Students have 3 housing options:
  1)On-Campus:  ASU Residence Halls: On-campus housing is EXTREMELY limited, and is assigned on a first-come, first-served basis!  Students should apply for on-campus housing as soon as they have the opportunity, but housing may fill before they have the chance to apply.
  2)Off-Campus:   Apartments: Prices are comparable to living on-campus, and apartments near campus are filled with ASU students. 
  3)Homestays: Homestays are not organized by ASU, but can be organized through a Homestay company in the area. 
Scholarships
Students applying for this university are automatically considered for KU’s Internationalisation scholarship.

Australian National University
Description
Founded in 1946, the Australian National University (ANU) supports a community of over 25,500 students. The ANU campus is located in Canberra, the capital of Australia and comprises seven academic colleges, which host a number of schools and research centres specialising in a variety of disciplines. The university is especially renowned for the strength of its Arts and Humanities and Natural Sciences departments. ANU courses are taught in small classes and state-of-the-art collaborative spaces that stimulate students to think differently. As ANU students, incoming exchange participants will have the support of our vibrant and talented community including access to academic, sporting, cultural and social activities, and access to comprehensive wellbeing support.
  ANU is located in the heart of Canberra, our beautiful and vibrant capital city. Canberra’s accessibility, friendliness, and overall quality of life makes it the perfect place to live, study and explore the region.
  Get a glimpse of a day in the life of an ANU exchange student!

Description
Barnard College of Columbia University was founded in 1889 and is devoted to empowering young women to pursue their passions. The Barnard community supports a community of 2,600 undergraduate women, all gathered on Campus Morningside Heights, situated in the heart of upper Manhattan.

The exchange agreement with Barnard is between DIS Copenhagen and Barnard College, and the exchange spots have been given to the University of Copenhagen by DIS. Students exchanged through the agreement should expect to attend an information meeting at DIS Copenhagen where you will meet students from Barnard studying in Copenhagen.

Charles University (Prague)
Description
Established in 1348, Charles University stands as one of the oldest and most esteemed universities in the world. Despite its rich historical legacy, the university maintains a forward-thinking and cosmopolitan approach, making it a modern and prestigious institution of higher education. As the largest and top-rated university in the Czech Republic, Charles University boasts 17 faculties, with 14 of them located in the vibrant city of Prague. The university's commitment to internationalization is evident in its diverse student body, with over 9,000 international students comprising approximately 20% of the total student population. This multicultural environment fosters a lively and dynamic atmosphere within the university.
Offering a wide range of courses in multiple languages, including English, German, French, and Czech, Charles University ensures that students from various backgrounds can pursue their academic goals. As an Erasmus student, one can benefit from the support of international associations dedicated to facilitating the integration and orientation process. Additionally, each faculty hosts various clubs and organizations, such as film clubs, choirs, and book clubs, providing students with opportunities to engage in extracurricular activities and explore their diverse interests. Charles University serves as a thriving hub of intellectual growth, cultural exchange, and community engagement, making it a highly sought-after institution for students seeking a comprehensive and enriching educational experience.
Prague offers a rich and stimulating environment for student life, combining historical depth with modern infrastructure. The city is compact and well-connected by public transport, including a metro system known for its depth and efficiency. Students can explore medieval streets, vibrant cultural venues, and green spaces throughout the city. A fun fact: Prague is home to the world’s largest castle complex (the Prague Castle) which spans over 70,000 square meters and includes palaces, churches, and historic lanes, making it a central part of the city’s identity and a frequent destination for students and visitors alike.

Description
The Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK) is a leading comprehensive research university. It has a total of 61 departments under the Faculties of Arts, Business Administration, Education, Engineering, Law, Medicine, Science, and Social Science. Currently, it has approximately 21,000 students undertaking undergraduate, postgraduate and doctoral programmes.
CUHK is the only university in Hong Kong with a college system. Each undergraduate student is associated with one of the nine Colleges. The Colleges are responsible for informal learning, and for academic and cultural immersion for students. CUHK is committed to general education and whole-person development.

Please note that availability of postgraduate-level courses for exchange students is very limited. Graduate students should make sure that they are allowed to take undergraduate courses while on exchange as part of their graduate degree before applying. 

All English-taught courses are open to exchange students except the following:
- Faculty of Medicine courses (except some biomedical sciences and public health courses)
- Faculty of Education courses (except some sports science and physical education (SPED) courses taught in English, and physical education (PHED) courses with an X after the course number)

MSc in Housing Studies programmes

programmes, except students from the exchange partner schools of these

There are also twenty-five research stations that offer postdoctoral fellowships.
Fudan now has an enrollment of 26,792 full-time degree candidates. Another 20,670 are studying at the schools of Continuing Education and Online Education. Besides, the University's population of foreign students is 2,812 today.

Description
Established in 1386, the University of Heidelberg holds the distinction of being the oldest university in Germany, embodying a legacy of knowledge and intellectual pursuit that spans centuries. With a community of over 31,000 students, including approximately 6,000 international students, it provides a vibrant and inclusive environment for higher education. Nestled by the picturesque lake Neckar, the campus offers a serene and inspiring backdrop for academic pursuits, fostering a conducive atmosphere for learning and personal growth.
Recognized as one of the strongest research universities in Europe, the institution boasts 12 faculties encompassing a diverse range of disciplines. These faculties, such as Biosciences, Chemistry and Earth Sciences, Mathematics and Computer Science, Physics and Astronomy, Philosophy, Modern Languages, Theology, Law, Economics and Social Sciences, Behavioral and Cultural Studies, and Medicine, offer comprehensive academic programs that cater to a wide array of interests and career paths. Through a commitment to rigorous research and innovation, the university cultivates an environment that fuels intellectual curiosity and advances knowledge in various fields.
Heidelberg is a compact and picturesque city located along the Neckar River in southwestern Germany. Known for its Baroque architecture, cobblestone streets, and vibrant cultural life, the city offers a student-friendly environment with efficient public transport and a strong cycling culture. The city’s historic center is home to cafés, bookstores, and cultural venues, all within walking distance of the university. A fun fact: tucked inside Heidelberg Castle is the world’s largest wine barrel, the Heidelberg Tun, built in 1751 with a capacity of over 219,000 liters—though it was only filled three times due to persistent leaks.

Description
HKUST - School of Science

The School of Science comprises 5 academic units that offer a wide variety of courses and programs in biological science, chemical science, mathematical science, ocean science and physical science.

Exchange students may take a combination of courses across different disciplines, with priority in taking courses offered by the School of Science. 
Language requirements
Proficient in English, as courses are mainly conducted in English
Restrictions
Course information is available at Course Catalog.

Course Restrictions
Generally speaking, science exchange students will have priority in taking science courses including chemistry, environmental science, life science, mathematics, ocean science and physics. For courses offered in other Schools, it will be subject to the availability of seats and approval of the course instructor. Due to limited seats, exchange students are unlikely to enroll in chemistry laboratory courses.
  Course Levels
Exchange students are expected to take mainly courses at undergraduate level. Science exchange students are allowed to take postgraduate courses subject to the requirements of the course pre-requisites (if any), the availability of seats and approval of the course instructor. Please note that postgraduate courses for exchange students are limited since most are under the self-financed programs which are not open to exchange students.

Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München (LMU Munich) is one of Europe’s foremost research universities, with a legacy of academic excellence dating back to its founding in 1472. LMU offers a broad spectrum of disciplines, ranging from the humanities and social sciences to medicine and natural sciences, and is consistently ranked among the top universities in Germany and internationally. Its programs are deeply rooted in cutting-edge research, and students benefit from close interaction with leading scholars and access to world-class facilities. With a strong international profile and a student body of over 50,000, LMU provides a stimulating academic environment that encourages intellectual curiosity and interdisciplinary collaboration.
Located in the heart of Munich, LMU offers students the opportunity to study in one of Germany’s most attractive and culturally rich cities. Munich is known for its high quality of life, vibrant arts scene, and proximity to the Alps, making it an ideal setting for both academic and personal growth. The city combines historic charm with modern innovation, and students can enjoy everything from museums and music festivals to outdoor activities and international cuisine. An exchange semester at LMU Munich promises not only academic enrichment but also a rewarding experience in one of Europe’s most welcoming and dynamic urban environments.

  Available NYU Schools & Faculties Open to Exchange Students through the NYU International Exchange Program:

Undergraduate Students

Graduate Students*
Graduate exchange students may apply to the faculties below, and their application must be accepted by the department to which they are applying. In reviewing the application, departments will be evaluating students based on academic achievement, as well as the applicant’s academic fit with the department in terms of past coursework taken and research interests.

Description
The Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) is Norway’s largest university, with about 43,500 students in total, including a significant number of international students. It has campuses in three cities: Trondheim (its main site), Gjøvik, and Ålesund. NTNU offers a wide variety of programmes across many disciplines: science, technology, engineering, health sciences, humanities, social sciences, etc. Many master’s programmes are taught entirely in English, making it quite accessible for exchange students who don’t want to speak Norwegian.
The location and surroundings of NTNU are a major part of its appeal. Trondheim, where the main campus is located, is a lively student city with a strong blend of history, culture, and nature. You get to enjoy river-side areas, a scenic fjord, forests nearby, islands, and plenty of opportunities for outdoor activities like hiking, skiing, or kayaking. The campuses themselves are fairly well integrated into the city, and public transport is good.

Courses at PUC-Rio are primarily taught in Portuguese, so students are generally expected to have sufficient proficiency in the language to follow their classes. However, PUC-Rio also offers a selection of courses taught in English, providing an excellent opportunity for students who may not speak Portuguese.

Although students are not required a specific level of proficiency in Portuguese language, all exchange students must take a placement test in order to confirm his/her/their knowledge of Portuguese, on the first day of classes. Four different levels are offered, from very basic to advanced level. Each student takes a written placement test on-line, and an oral one on the first day of classes. Tuition payment required.

Description
The history of Ritsumeikan dates back to 1869 when Prince Kinmochi Saionji, an eminent international statesman of modern Japan, founded "Ritsumeikan" as a private academy on the site of the Kyoto Imperial Palace. Today, Ritsumeikan University offers a wide range of courses in advanced studies at its Kinugasa Campus in Kyoto, Biwako‐Kusatsu Campus (BKC) in Shiga and Osaka‐Ibaraki Campus (OIC) in Osaka.
The Study in Kyoto Program (SKP) brings together students from around the world. It offers numerous courses in Japanese language and culture, utilizing the extensive historical and cultural resources of the ancient city of Kyoto. The program also offers courses in English regarding both Japan‐related and business subjects. 
  Kyoto, the capital of Japan from 794 to 1868, is an ideal place for study, being a city with one of the highest concentrations of academic institutions in Japan. Kyoto is home to some of Japan's most important World Heritage Sites such as the Ryoanji, Ninnaji and Kinkakuji (Golden Pavilion) Temples, which are located right at the doorstep of Ritsumeikan University. It enjoys a harmonious blend of the ancient and the modern, thus giving students the unique opportunity to experience all aspects of Japanese society. 
  By studying in SKP at Ritsumeikan University, students are able to take advantage of the rich academic and cultural environment of this city, leading to a truly fruitful and memorable study experience in Japan.

Restrictions: 
- Colleges of Medicine/Pharmacy/Dentistry/Nursing/Music/Fine Arts/Global MBA are usually not available to exchange students.
- College of Business Administration (CBA) is restricted to students who are currently majoring in business or economics at home university. CBA has very limited capacity for exchange students and rejections are to be expected.
- Dept. of Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) has limited capacity for undergraduate exchange students. Undergraduate applicants for CSE must have earned at least 15 credits in CSE-related courses (one credit corresponds to 15 lecture hours or 30 lab hours). Acceptance is competitive and rejections to be expected.The past few years UCPH students applying to CSE have been rejected. 
- Dept. of Vocal Music is restricted to students who are currently studying vocal music at home university.
- College of Medicine is NO longer available to exchange students.
- School of Dentistry is open only to graduate students currently enrolled in a Master’s program at School of Dentistry or the equivalent at home university.
- Graduate School of Business (MBA program) is restricted to current full-time MBA applicants at home university.

Restrictions and Considerations
  For students who wants to apply for graduate studies at SFU:
 The vast majority of exchange students at SFU are admitted as undergraduates. At Simon Fraser University, undergraduate degrees are generally four years in length. This means that many exchange students who are graduate students at their home universities may actually be better suited to take undergraduate Upper-Division courses (300 and 400 level) at SFU, especially if they are in the first year of their graduate degrees.  Note that being admitted as a graduate student may impact which SFU Residence building for which you are eligible.

Generally, UCPH strongly recommends the higher level C1 (advanced proficiency in French) when studying at Sorbonne University - to make sure that you can cope sufficiently in courses taught in French. 
Almost all courses and exams are in held French. In English, it is rarely possible to find a full study load of 30 ECTS. If you are studying at their Faculty of Science and Engineering, there may be a chance of finding enough courses in English, though experience tells us that this is not always the case.  
Study load for undergraduate students
30 ECTS
Study load for graduate students
30 ECTS
Restrictions
The Master students can take BA and MA level courses, but the Bachelor students can only take BA level courses. If BA students are interested in a MA level course, they must ask the academic coordinator and the professor of the MA course if they can take this course.
Most courses are taught in French, with the exception of certain language departments where courses are taught in both the learned language and French. It is very unlikely that student can find a full study load in English and must therefore be very proficient in French.
You must be able to get a pre-approval of 30 ECTS from UCPH per semester.
  Restrictions for UCPH-students:
- Political Science-students (Statskundskab) at BA-level cannot find courses at Sorbonne for their semester 5
- There are no courses in Economics at Sorbonne
- There are no courses in Law at Sorbonne
  Course information
On this exchange agreement you may look for interesting study areas with the two faculties below:
Faculté de Lettres/Faculty of Arts and Humanities - discover the Schools and departments
Course catalogue for Faculty of Arts and Humanities: https://moodle-lettres-24.sorbonne-universite.fr/course/view.php?id=403#section-1. When you have chosen e.g. 'lettres' then press 'Tout dépliér' as it may give you access to additional info. 
Incoming Erasmus students must take at least 50% of their workload inside their main department. The rest of their workload can be made up of courses from other departments within this Faculty.
  Questions to Faculty of Humanities can be sent to; lettres-erasmus-inri@sorbonne-universite.fr
    Faculté des Sciences et IngénieriFaculty of Science & Engineering - se info on study areas.

Course catalogue for Faculty of Science & Engineering: https://sciences.sorbonne-universite.fr/formation-sciences/offre-de-formation and list of English courses at FSI can be found here; https://sciences.sorbonne-universite.fr/en/study/our-programmes
  Questions to Faculty of Science and Engineering; sciences-dfipve-mobilite-in@sorbonne-universite.fr
  Later on at the nomination process, you must be assigned to a specific department and study area within the specific faculty. 
  Be aware that Sorbonne has different campuses all over the city, so remember to check the transportation time if you take classes in different campuses the same day.
Important links
Information for exchange students
  See study areas within the Faculty of Arts and Humanities: https://formations-lettres.sorbonne-universite.fr//fr/index.html

Housing
Sorbonne University does offer some accommodation for exchange students; however, it is important to note that they do not guarantee accommodation, so international students are recommend to look for private housing when possible. Students receive information about housing when nominated for Erasmus.
Consider Fondation Danoise in Cité Universitaire. Accommodation in general is very expensive in Paris, and difficult to find.

Students applying for this university are automatically nominated for the Erasmus+ scholarship.

Description
UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

Description
The Federal University of Rio de Janeiro – UFRJ has three campi: Cidade Universitária, Praia Vermellha and Macaé, comprising institutes, schools, faculties and supplementary units, which include seven museums, among which we can highlight the National Museum, eight university hospitals, and the third largest ocean basin in the world for research on offshore oil exploration.

The university has partnerships with several national and multinational enterprises, some of which have facilities on the very UFRJ campi. It is the case of Petrobras, whose biggest research center is located on Cidade Universitária campus. Following a global trend, the UFRJ establishes and maintains cooperative relationships with institutions from all continents, which now amounts to over 200 international institutions all over the world.

Description
University College Cork (UCC) is one of Ireland’s leading universities, known for its strong academic reputation and commitment to research and innovation. It serves a diverse student body of over 24,000 and offers a wide range of disciplines across its faculties. UCC places a strong emphasis on sustainability, international collaboration, and student-centered learning. The university integrates research with teaching and encourages students to engage with real-world challenges through interdisciplinary approaches and community partnerships. Its campus combines historic architecture with modern facilities, creating a balanced environment for academic and personal development.
Cork, located in the south of Ireland, is a lively and welcoming city with a rich cultural heritage and a strong student presence. The city is compact and easy to navigate, with good public transport and a wide range of cafés, music venues, and cultural events. Students can explore nearby coastal areas, historic sites, and scenic countryside, all within easy reach. A fun fact: Cork is home to one of the world’s largest natural harbours and also produces a significant share of the world’s Tic Tacs.

Description
The University of Agder is a modern, dynamic university in southern Norway with about 14,000 students, spread mainly over two campuses in Kristiansand and Grimstad. While many of the undergraduate courses are taught in Norwegian, there is a solid selection of Master’s‐level and exchange courses in English. The Grimstad campus is especially noted for its engineering, technology, and applied sciences orientation, with modern facilities, while Kristiansand is larger, more central, and offers greater diversity in programme choice.
The cities where UiA is based Kristiansand and Grimstad combine the charms of Southern Norway’s coastal landscape with good student amenities and fairly easy living. Kristiansand is the larger city, with a compact centre, decent public transit, green areas, and recreational options close by: forests, beaches, hiking, and water sports. Many services, cafés, shops, and cultural venues are within walking distance if you live in or near the city centre or close to campus, which makes day-to-day life convenient. Grimstad is smaller and the distance from campus to town is short.

Description
University of California is an undergraduate system agreement with nine campus locations all part of the University of California Education Abroad Program (UCEAP):
1) Berkeley (UCB)
2) Davis (UCD)
3) Irvine (UCI)
4) Los Angeles (UCLA)
5) Merced (UCM)
6) Riverside (UCR)
7) San Diego (UCSD)
8) Santa Barbara (UCSB)
9) Santa Cruz (UCSC)
You cannot apply to the individual UC campuses in your application for exchange. Students apply for admission to 3 UC campuses after nomination for exchange.
Academic calendar
Berkeley & Merced (semester universities)
Fall semester: August-December
Spring semester: January-May

Davis, Irvine, Los Angeles, Riverside, San Diego, Santa Barbara & Santa Cruz (quarter universities):

Full academic year: September-June
Fall + winter quarter: September-March
Winter + spring quarter: January-June
  Please note that the spring semester begins very early compared to the spring semester at UCPH.
You should only apply for the spring semester, if you do not have exams in January at UCPH.
GPA for undergraduate admission
7 (Danish scale)
GPA for graduate admission
N/A
Accepted proof of language proficiency (required AFTER nomination)
English-Language Proficiency Examinations
Study load for undergraduate students
Berkeley & Merced: 13 units (semester units) = 30 ECTS

Davis, Irvine, Los Angeles, Riverside, San Diego, Santa Barbara & Santa Cruz (quarter campuses): 18 units (quarter units) = 30 ECTS

- Immigration requirement: To meet the immigration requirements, all students at the above mentioned quarter campuses must enroll in 12 units per quarter. As the duration of an exchange stay is at least two quarters, students must enroll in courses corresponding to 24 units, which is more than the 18 units required to obtain 30 ECTS. Transfer of excess units to ECTS will be handled according to the rules of the relevant UCPH faculty (due to the block structure at the Faculty of Science at UCPH, students from this faculty may consider taking 27 units, as this would give them the opportunity to transfer 45 ECTS before they return for block 4).
Restrictions
This agreement is for undergraduate studies only. Students who have passed  240 ECTS or more at university level by the time of departure cannot apply for this agreement.
  You have to apply for a minimum of two quarters or one semester. If you apply for fall, your exchange period will last from September to March.
Spring semester from January to June.

There are 9 UC campuses in total. After your nomination from UCPH, you will start your application to UC. You must apply to at least three different campuses, and the University of California Education Abroad programme (UCEAP) will decide which campus you are placed at.

University of California health insurance is mandatory.

Majors closed for exchange:
- Business Administration
- Design
- Journalism
- Medicine, Nursing, veterinary medicine
- Law
- Limited access to courses in Economics, Business and Computer Science

You must select a UC major for your application.
- Your major is the subject area where you will spend the majority of your time studying.
- This might be a different major from your major at UCPH.
- Plan to enroll in two or three courses in your major department each term, with the remainder of your classes in other majors. To do so you must meet the course prerequisites and space must be available in the course.

Proof of financial resources when applying for a J1-student exchange visitor visa is required after acceptance to the University of California.
  You cannot apply for a second time
Incoming students can only apply for exchange for a maximum of 1 academic year in total due to UCEAP restrictions. 
At UC, this is either 3 quarters or 2 semesters. 
This means, that if you have already been on exchange at UC from KU, you will not fulfill the requirements for attending UC for 2 quarters or 1 semester, as they cannot send you to the same campus again, nor guarantee that your application for a specific campus will be successful.

UC Courses
Housing
All campuses have housing offices where you can apply for on-campus housing. Many campuses have special housing for international students, often named International House. If you do not wish to live on-campus, many campuses have an off-campus housing office to assist you in finding accommodation off-campus.
Scholarships
Students applying for this university are automatically considered for KU’s Internationalisation scholarship.
Additional information
Fall semester applicants: If you want to apply for the fall semester at this university, you need to apply in the first application round (with deadline in November), as this is the only time fall semester spots will be offered.
  If you are potentially interested in studying at UC for more than 1 semester
we encourage you to apply for 2 semesters right away. It is easier to withdraw from a semester than extending your stay.

Established in 1873 by scholars from Oxford and Cambridge, the University of Canterbury, in Christchurch, New Zealand, has an international reputation for academic excellence in both teaching and research. Description
Situated between the Alps and the Jura mountains, at the southwestern tip of Switzerland and Lake Geneva, Geneva is in the heart of Western Europe. Its ideal geographical location has made the city a prosperous hub for trade and exchanges of all kinds. This cosmopolitan city is home to more than 180 nationalities and is recognized worldwide for its spirit of openness and tolerance.
Considered the smallest of the major world capitals, Geneva, which is home to some thirty international organizations including the UN, WTO, WHO and ILO and more than 300 NGOs and permanent missions of foreign states, has a true international vocation.This multicultural environment is reflected at the University of Geneva, where about one third of the students are foreign nationals. The university pursues a very active policy of international relations; in particular, it participates in numerous bi- or multilateral agreements, networks and programs such as Erasmus and Socrates.
Ideally located near the most famous ski areas of the Alps, not far from the Mediterranean, while being anchored in Central Europe, Geneva offers the rare advantages of a cosmopolitan city on a human scale. Cuisine and music from all over the world are found here. Lovers of green spaces will find an atmosphere conducive to meditation in its parks or on the shores of Lake Geneva. Culture lovers will be spoilt for choice, thanks to the presence in Geneva of one of the most prestigious opera scenes in Europe, several film festivals, and numerous concert halls and theaters.
The University of Geneva takes an active part in this cultural scene. Its film club and choir in particular attract large audiences. But Geneva also lives to the rhythm of a multitude of associations, representing the most diverse countries and interests, which make it uniquely suited for the exchange of ideas and traditions.

Read more about accommodation here.
Geneva offers different kinds of accommodation. In Geneva acocmmodation is extremely limited. Make sure to get a head start! The quicker you start the process, the better.
In your letter of acceptance you will receive links to apply online for accommodation. Complete the online forms as soon as possible.
Important: this does not guarantee that you will be attributed a room.
It is difficult and expensive to find accommodation in Geneva. The University Housing Office (“Bureau des Logements”- BLOG) and “Cité Universitaire” provide student residences. Due to a high demand and low availability, there is no guarantee that all the applications receive an offer.
IMPORTANT: Please note that the BLOG will give priority for students requiring a visa to come as well as for students coming from university member of the 4EU+ Alliance.

Restrictions
Restricted study areas: Medicine, Veterinary Medicine, and Dentistry.
Media and Communications students   
Limited availability. Leeds may be able to accept a limited number of students that have a major in media and/ or communication. Students  may only take a limited number of courses in this subject area - and must therefore also consider courses from other teaching schools at Leeds.
Important information about provisional results and receiving your transcript of records (karakterudskrift) from Leeds:
Semester 1 / autumn semester
Even if semester 1 ends in January, Leeds cannot produce an official transcript of records (karakterudskrift) until the end of July. This is because the January exam results remain provisional until external exam board meetings in late June. University of Leeds will instead provide a provisional transcript for all students who are studying at Leeds for semester 1. This may delay your final transfer of credits at UCPH, as not all faculties accept provisional transcript of records for credit transfer. 
Postgraduate courses
Academic transcripts for postgraduate modules are not released until November after studies have ended at Leeds. This means, students going on exchange in e.g the autumn semester, will not be able to transfer credits earned at Leeds before November in the subsequent academic year. 
Mix of undergraduate and postgraduate courses
If you choose a mixture of undergraduate and postgraduate modules, Leeds will send you a transcript in July, but the postgraduate results will be provisional until November and could change. Leeds will automatically send you another transcript in November, which will show all your official results.
Please also note that provisional marks/results rarely change.
    Course information
  About module selection 

Students should complete the module enrolment form sent in their acceptance email with their preferred module codes. The Teaching Schools will then enrol them on the modules.

When the University of Leeds accept you to the study abroad programme, you will be classed as undergraduate, but this does not prevent you from selecting postgraduate modules as Leeds have a postgraduate agreement with UCPH.

If applying for postgraduate module(s), applicants will need to show that they meet the module’s prerequisites on their academic transcript.

If you would like to know in advance whether or not it will be possible for you to study some specific postgraduate level modules, you can contact the relevant teaching school - please remember to include your transcript of records in the email. Please find a list of the schools' study abroad coordinator contacts here.

Law:

Computer Science:
Computer Science course units are very heavily oversubscribed. 
History:
Most Level 3 course units are heavily oversubscribed, particularly Modern History courses. Study Abroad / exchange students who are majoring in History and already have an extensive background in the subject may be considered for Level 3 course units, but will be subject to an interview on arrival, and a place on a Level 3 course unit cannot be guaranteed. Students wishing to access Level 2 History courses should also have sufficient background study in the subject. History majors who plan to join us for Semester 2 of the academic year and who wish to take Level 3 History course units need to make an early application.  Manchester recommends applying by 1 June.
Music:
All Music course units have prerequisites. Students must be a music major to be eligible for consideration for these course units. 
Psychology:
Level 3 Psychology courses are only open to students majoring in Psychology, in years 3 or 4 of their undergraduate degree, with a GPA of 3.3 or above.
Psychology – students cannot take the Year 2 unit PSYC21061 Statistics and Data Analysis if they will not be available for the semester 1 exam period.
Psychology – students with no previous Psychology training in Research Methods and Statistics cannot take the following Year 2 units: Cognition, Topics & Issues in Social Psychology, Cognitive Neuroscience, Interventions to Improve Mental Health & Wellbeing. 
Creative Writing:
Students applying for Level 3 Creative Writing course units must supply an example of written work (eg a poem or a story). You should email this piece of writing to exchangestudy@manchester.ac.uk after receiving your offer letter.

Students have access to a variety of sports at affordable rates, both within the center's events and in affiliated sports facilities. Moreover, the university has established a network of relationships and signed agreements with cultural institutions and associations in Milan and other cities. This allows for vibrant cultural exchanges between the academic and local communities, providing students with discounts at museums, theaters, cinemas, and more, further enhancing their educational experience. Through its commitment to research, education, and the holistic development of its students, the University of Milan remains at the forefront of academic excellence and actively contributes to the intellectual, cultural, and social fabric of both Milan and the wider academic community.

Description
The University of Oslo is Norway’s oldest and largest university, founded in 1811, and today home to around 27,000 students. There are more than 800 courses taught in English across all faculties, making it quite accessible for exchange students. The university is spread across several campuses: the major one is Blindern, but there are additional facilities at places like Ullevål and Tøyen. 
Oslo, the capital of Norway, offers a unique combination of urban life and natural beauty, making it an attractive destination for students. The city is surrounded by forests and fjords, with outdoor activities like hiking, skiing, and kayaking easily accessible via public transport. Oslo is known for its cultural diversity, vibrant arts scene, and commitment to sustainability. Students can enjoy museums, music festivals, and a lively café culture, all within a compact and walkable city center.

- Conversion and levels: Study load is calculated according to the level of the specific course. Please note that not all UCPH Master’s (graduate) programmes allow credit transfer of bachelor (undergraduate) courses into their programmes.
- Exceptions to the conversion: Conversion does not apply to mandatory stays (HUM) or clinical electives (SUND) abroad.
Restrictions
Special Requirements and Course Restrictions:
The School of Engineering (Escola Politécnica) requires exchange students to enroll in a number of subjects that correspond to 12 credits per semester.
The Ribeirão Preto School of Economics, Administration, and Accounting (Faculdade de Economia e Administração de Ribeirão Preto – FEARP) requires applicants to enroll in a number of subjects that correspond to at least 16 credits.
The School of Architecture and Urbanism (Faculdade de Arquitetura e Urbanismo – FAU) and the School of Economics, Business, and Accountancy (Faculdade de Economia, Administração, Contabilidade e Atuária – FEA) receive a large number of applications each semester and have limited capacity to admit all exchange students. Please refer to their Local International Offices before nominating students/applying. It is highly recommended that students intending to apply for the above-mentioned schools consider the possibility of applying to other schools as well.
Important links
Website
Academic Calendar

Housing
There are no accommodation options for exchange students within the campuses. Search for accommodation must be carried out by the students themselves, according to their economic conditions and interests. USP strongly recommends that students take part in the USP iFriends program in order to get more information about accommodation. Applicants may opt to take part in the buddy program when filling out the online application form.
Scholarships
Students applying for this university are automatically considered for KU’s Internationalisation scholarship.
Additional information
To be eligible for a KU exchange spot, students must have completed at least one year of study (equivalent to 60 ECTS) by the time they apply for admission to the host university. This must be documented on their transcript by the time of applying to the host university.

First-year bachelor students should note that applications to the host university are submitted before the start of each semester — typically between March and April for the autumn semester and between September and November for the spring semester.

First-year master's students are considered to meet this requirement based on their completed bachelor's degree.

Students who wish to take courses taught in Japanese need JLPT Level N1. The different departments may have different requirements for the minimum level. Students who want to take courses taught in Japanese should check very early in the application process when there are available test dates for the JLPT in Denmark. Restrictions
First and second year undergraduate students are not accepted for USTEP. For students enrolled in three-year undergraduate programs, they must have finished at least 1.5-year (3 semesters) of their degree programme at the time of their arrival at UTokyo.

Students are advised to check the course catalogue about which language their preferred courses are taught in.

Type G applicants must in addition to the general requirements also meet the conditions set by the Graduate School they intend to apply for: Prerequisites for USTEP Type G Applicants (u-tokyo.ac.jp)
Course information
Exchange students can apply to the USTEP Type U (undergraduate) or USTEP Type G (graduate) program depending on their purpose of participation and/or their proficiency in the English and/or Japanese language. 

Type U is designed for undergraduate students to take various courses given in English and/or Japanese in the various Faculties/College in accordance with their English and/or Japanese language proficiency.

Type G is designed for graduate students. Type G participants will belong to one of the Graduate Schools to which they are admitted and will be able to take various courses or conduct research under the guidance of a faculty advisor. Type G is only open to graduate students.
  Important information for graduate students: Type G applicants are expected to submit a detailed and well-developed study plan to UTokyo, ensuring that it is well aligned with the course offerings of the intended Graduate School and the research expertise of the department and faculty member(s). Failure to do so may result in rejection. Graduate students should only apply for this agreement, if they are able to develop an excellent study plan that meets the expectations described above, or if they are able to obtain a pre-approval of undergraduate courses abroad as part of their graduate degree program at UCPH, in which case, they should consider applying as Type U exchange students.

Restrictions
The agreement is for undergraduate studies only. All undergraduate exchange students will be admitted through the College of Arts & Sciences. 
If you are a graduate student at University of Copenhagen when you go abroad, you need to be able to transfer undergraduate courses to your degree at UCPH as you will not be allowed to follow graduate courses. 
If you read reports from previous exchange students at UW, you might see that they were graduate students and took graduate courses - the rules have changed since then and from 2023 the agreement is only open for undergraduate studies. 

It is not possible to apply for the fall semester only. You can either apply for the fall +winter quarter (September-March), the winter + spring quarter (January-June), or the full academic year (September-June). This is one of the very few exchange agreements outside Europe where you can apply for a full academic year abroad.

Description
Ca’ Foscari University of Venice, established in 1868 as Italy’s first business school, has evolved into a comprehensive institution offering a wide array of academic programs. With approximately 20,000 students and eight departments, the university is structured around four main academic areas: Foreign Languages and Literatures, Humanities, Economics, and Sciences. Exchange students have access to a diverse selection of courses, including subjects in international business, cultural studies, environmental science, and linguistics, many of which are offered in English. Ca’ Foscari maintains a strong emphasis on global engagement and intercultural exchange, providing international students with academic experiences deeply connected to the university’s historical strengths in commerce, languages, and cultural heritage.
Situated in the heart of Venice, Ca’ Foscari’s campuses are embedded within the city’s iconic canals and historic architecture. The unique urban landscape offers students a learning environment that blends academic life with the artistic, cultural, and maritime traditions of Venice. From exploring local markets and museums to participating in university-organized cultural events, exchange students experience daily life in a city renowned for its beauty, history, and vibrant student atmosphere. The University also offers Italian language courses for international students and a ‘buddy’ program with local students.

École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales
Description
École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales (EHESS) is a distinguished graduate-only research institution, founded in 1984 as a grand établissement alongside the Collège de France and Sciences Po. With its main campus in Paris and additional sites in Marseille, Lyon, and Toulouse, EHESS is internationally recognized for its interdisciplinary approach to the social sciences and humanities. The university has been home to some of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, including Pierre Bourdieu, Claude Lévi-Strauss, and Jacques Derrida. Today, EHESS hosts a diverse student body of approximately 3,000 students from around the world, drawn by its unique academic environment that combines rigorous historical analysis with contemporary research.
Situated in central Paris, EHESS offers students an exceptional opportunity to study in one of Europe’s foremost intellectual and cultural capitals. The university’s location places students at the heart of a city renowned for its libraries, archives, museums, and vibrant academic life. Paris provides a stimulating backdrop for scholarly engagement, with countless opportunities to attend public lectures, exhibitions, and cultural events. For students seeking a rich academic experience alongside immersion in a dynamic urban setting, EHESS represents an ideal destination for an exchange semester.

École Normale Supérieure de Lyon
Description
The École Normale Supérieure de Lyon (ENS de Lyon) is a highly selective French public institution focused on advanced education and research in the sciences and humanities. It hosts approximately 2,300 students and over 500 faculty and researchers. ENS de Lyon is part of the network of Écoles normales supérieures, known for preparing students for careers in academia and public service. The institution is distinguished by its strong emphasis on research-led teaching and its integration with national research bodies such as CNRS and INRIA. Its compact size and rigorous academic environment foster close interaction between students and researchers, offering a setting that differs from larger, more generalist universities.
ENS de Lyon is located in the city of Lyon, France’s third-largest urban area and a major centre for education, culture, and innovation. The university’s campuses are situated in the Gerland district, which is well connected by public transport and close to both the Rhône river and the city’s central areas. Lyon combines historical architecture with modern infrastructure and is known for its role in the humanities, sciences, and gastronomy. The city offers a manageable scale, active student life, and access to cultural institutions, making it a practical and engaging place to spend an exchange semester. Its location in southeastern France also provides convenient links to other European cities and regions.

Description
Erasmus University Rotterdam is a leading international research university known for its strong focus on societal impact and academic excellence. Established in 1913, the university offers a wide range of programs in economics, business, law, social sciences, health, and humanities. It is particularly renowned for its Erasmus School of Economics and Rotterdam School of Management, both of which enjoy global recognition. With a student population of over 30,000, including a large international community, Erasmus University fosters an intellectually stimulating and diverse academic environment. Its interdisciplinary approach and emphasis on real-world relevance make it an ideal destination for students seeking a forward-thinking and globally connected exchange experience.
Located in the vibrant city of Rotterdam, the university offers students the opportunity to live and study in one of Europe’s most innovative urban centers. Rotterdam is known for its modern architecture, dynamic cultural scene, and status as a major international port city. The city’s youthful energy, excellent public transport, and wide range of cultural and recreational activities make it especially appealing to students. Whether exploring contemporary art museums, attending music festivals, or enjoying the city’s waterfront, exchange students will find Rotterdam to be a lively and inspiring place to complement their academic journey at Erasmus University.

Established in 1913, the university offers a wide range of programs in economics, business, law, social sciences, health, and humanities. It is particularly renowned for its Erasmus School of Economics and Rotterdam School of Management, both of which enjoy global recognition. With a student population of over 30,000, including a large international community, Erasmus University fosters an intellectually stimulating and diverse academic environment. Its interdisciplinary approach and emphasis on real-world relevance make it an ideal destination for students seeking a forward-thinking and globally connected exchange experience.
Located in the vibrant city of Rotterdam, the university offers students the opportunity to live and study in one of Europe’s most innovative urban centers. Rotterdam is known for its modern architecture, dynamic cultural scene, and status as a major international port city. The city’s youthful energy, excellent public transport, and wide range of cultural and recreational activities make it especially appealing to students. Whether exploring contemporary art museums, attending music festivals, or enjoying the city’s waterfront, exchange students will find Rotterdam to be a lively and inspiring place to complement their academic journey at Erasmus University.

HKUST Academic Calendar

Restrictions
  Course information
Exchange students can select SHSS courses as long as they meet the prerequisite requirements and subject to space availabilities. Some courses may require professor approval for enrollment. Courses offered by SHSS are identified by the following prefixes in the course codes: HUMA, SOSC, SHSS 
Undergraduate (UG) / Postgraduate (PG) course offering varies by semester. Below past year course is for reference purpose only:
Social Science Courses:
UG https://prog-crs.hkust.edu.hk/ugcourse/2024-25/SOSC 
PG https://prog-crs.hkust.edu.hk/pgcourse/2024-25/SOSC 
Humanities Courses:
UG https://prog-crs.hkust.edu.hk/ugcourse/2024-25/HUMA 
PG https://prog-crs.hkust.edu.hk/pgcourse/2024-25/HUMA 
Language Courses:
UG / PG https://cle.hkust.edu.hk/courses 
  Restrictions
- SSMA, MGCS, MALS, MALE, HMMA - Students who are not enrolled in these special programs normally are not allowed to register for these courses.
- ACCT, ECON, FINA, ISOM, MARK, MGMT - Due to availabilities and capacity reserved to HKUST business degree seeking students. Business courses at postgraduate (PG) level (i.e. 5000-level or above) are reserved for special business programs and are closed to students who are not in the program. Therefore, exchange students admitted to the School of Humanities and Social Science should NOT plan on taking business courses at PG level.

Enrollment to courses outside SHSS (for example, business) is not available.

Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

Description
Jeju National University (JNU) is the only state-designated Flagship Korean National University on Jeju, an island that has been awarded UNESCO’s triple crown in the field of natural science and was dubbed a New Seven Wonders of Nature. Since its opening in 1952, JNU has worked to cultivate talent who will change the future under the vision of “the university faithful to the basics and the university prepared for the future.”
The nation’s university assessment has named JNU as having the best educational environment in terms of the ratio of full-time professors to students and per capita educational expenses. This proves that JNU has advanced its educational environment to the next level. JNU runs 14 colleges, 1 graduate school, 3 professional graduate schools and 6 specialized graduate schools. Particularly, it boasts of well-established teacher’s colleges for potential primary and secondary teachers and a comprehensive medical education system that involves colleges specialized in medicine, pharmacy, nursing and veterinary medicine.
To nurture global talent, JNU runs a customized curriculum that offers students all types of courses from fundamental to advanced levels. Currently, JNU has 1,500 international students from nearly 40 countries and networks with an estimated 300 universities from more than 50 countries including the United States. It has fully established this network of global education and students are encouraged to participate in its overseas internship program, intensive English education program and student exchange program.

Description
KIMEP University is a leading, innovative institution in Central Asia that promotes educational excellence through teaching and research. When Kazakhstan became independent in 1991, the authorities realized the pressing need for Western-style education as a means to catalyze and sustain economic and social change. As part of this vision, KIMEP was conceived and founded. From its modest beginnings as a small institution, it has grown into a world-renowned university.
KIMEP University has established a tradition of excellence by offering programs of the highest academic quality that follow international standards and best practices. The correspondence of our standards to those in Europe and the United States is underlined by the accreditation of many of our programs by international educational quality assurance agencies.
The university prides itself on more than simply providing first-class academic programs. KIMEP University distinguishes itself in its devotion to the well-being and personal development of its students. In stark contrast to many universities in Central Asia, KIMEP University involves representatives from its independent student government in decision-making processes and university committees. This complements the university’s broader commitment to transparency, fairness, and integrity, as well as fosters a sense of empowerment and responsibility among students.
The alumni which the university produces are not only highly qualified, but deeply committed to these values, and it is this characteristic that makes them such desirable hires. They are recruited by top local and international firms and organizations, and they can be found in diverse professional roles around the world.
KIMEP University has played a critical role in the economic modernization of Kazakhstan. Our alumni include many of Kazakhstan’s political and business leaders. KIMEP University serves as an incubator for future generations of Kazakhstani and regional leaders. As an innovative and adaptive institution, KIMEP University will continue to tailor its services to the needs of its students and to those of the Kazakhstani state and society as a whole.

A fun fact: Leiden is the birthplace of Rembrandt van Rijn and also home to the world’s oldest university observatory, established in 1633—making it a city where art and science have long flourished side by side.

Restrictions
BA and MA students can apply for this agreement.
The Institute for European Ethnology and Cultural Analysis at LMU Munich offers the complete range of academic subjects in European Ethnology, extending from the analysis of cultural phenomena on our doorstep to the investigation of cultural phenomena in other European societies, and including the processes of Europeanization, transnationalization, and migration, as well as cultural comparison. Incoming Erasmus students can also choose English courses from other institutes (for example, from Sociology, Cultural Anthropology, English Studies).
Additional restrictions:
Erasmus students must choose at least 60% of their course modules in the field of study defined in the bilateral agreement. Students should take the other 40% of their course modules in no more than two other fields of study. The Faculties of Business Administration, Medicine, Dentistry, Veterinary Medicine, Pharmacy do not accept students outside of their own inter-Institutional agreements. Likewise, places cannot be guaranteed in Sociology and English taught Politics courses.Therefore, all exchange students can choose courses in a maximum of three study areas.
If you have no previous knowledge in a given subject area, you can only attend introductory courses in that subject area.
If you are studying in a bachelor's program, you are generally not allowed to take master's courses (LMU will discuss exceptions to this rule internally)

Description
Osnabrück University is a mid-sized public university in northwestern Germany, with a student population of around 14,000 and a staff of approximately 1,200 academic employees. It is known for its interdisciplinary research and strong collaboration between faculties, particularly in the humanities, social sciences, and natural sciences. The university places a strong emphasis on international cooperation and offers a welcoming environment for exchange students. One of its distinctive features is the close integration of research and teaching, supported by a compact campus structure that fosters interaction across disciplines.
The university is located in the city of Osnabrück, a historic and student-friendly city in Lower Saxony. With a population of just over 160,000, Osnabrück offers a manageable and affordable urban environment, while still providing a rich cultural life, green surroundings, and good public transport. The city has a strong academic and youthful character, shaped in part by its university community. Its location between the Teutoburg Forest and the Wiehen Hills also makes it attractive for students who enjoy outdoor activities alongside their studies.

Incoming EuMIGS Double Degree-students will follow a specialisation in ‘migration regimes in practice’. They will be trained in studying migration, migration-related diversity and processes of inclusion as contested issues and objects of regulation that are observed, defined, structured and ‘produced’ by various actors, institutions, discourses, and practices – and also by migration research itself.
Specific foci within the programme include:
the institutional structures, policies and actors of migration regimes as regulatory systems in a cross-national and historical perspective
the history of modern migration
refugee migration (regimes)
gender and migration
the spatial dimension of migration (regimes)
academic knowledge production on migration

Pompeu Fabra University (UPF), founded in 1990, is a modern public university located in the heart of Barcelona. It serves a student population of around 12,000 and is known for its strong focus on research, innovation, and internationalization. UPF offers programmes across social sciences, humanities, communication, health, and life sciences, and maintains a high level of academic performance and global engagement. The university emphasizes interdisciplinary learning and is closely connected to local and international research networks. Its compact urban campuses foster a dynamic and accessible academic environment.
Barcelona provides an ideal setting for student life, combining Mediterranean climate, rich cultural heritage, and excellent public transport. The city is known for its architecture, beaches, and vibrant neighborhoods, all easily reachable by metro, tram, or bike. Students benefit from a wide range of cultural events, cafés, and outdoor activities throughout the year. A fun fact: Barcelona is home to one of the few beaches in the world that was artificially created for the Olympics—before 1992, the city had no real beach culture. Today, it’s a central part of student life and a symbol of the city’s transformation.

Sungkyunkwan University was founded at the beginning of the Joseon Dynasty in 1398 as the highest national educational institute in the early years of the Joseon Dynasty in Korea. As the oldest university in East Asia, it has fostered leaders of our society for over 600 years.

Sungkyunkwan University, continuously strives to integrate its founding tradition philosophy with the modern technology in order with a view to cultivating global graduate’ s talents who will lead the 21st century.

What the university has achieved in the past fifty years meets and exceeds that of the preceding history. We take pride in the university's innovative spirit; as a result of the long efforts to place natural sciences on an equal footing with our strong foundation in humanities, we now operate two campuses: the Humanities and Social Sciences Campus and the Natural Sciences Campus.

Université de Neuchâtel
Description
Université de Neuchâtel is a public university in western Switzerland with a student body of approximately 4,000 and a staff of around 1,200. It offers a compact academic environment with four faculties and a strong emphasis on research, particularly in law, economics, and natural sciences. The university is known for its bilingual setting (French and English), which supports international collaboration and mobility. Its manageable size allows for close interaction between students and faculty, and its location in the French-speaking part of Switzerland provides access to cross-border academic networks.
The university is located in the city of Neuchâtel, situated on the shores of Lake Neuchâtel and surrounded by the Jura mountains. The city is known for its watchmaking heritage and offers a mix of historical architecture and natural landscapes. Neuchâtel is walkable, well-connected by rail, and offers a calm setting for study with access to outdoor activities year-round. A small curiosity: the lake next to the city is the largest entirely within Swiss borders, and its water is so clear that it’s used as a benchmark for measuring lake transparency across Europe.

Description
The University of Pisa, established in 1343, is one of Italy’s most prestigious public universities, with a long-standing tradition in scientific and humanistic research. It currently hosts over 50,000 students and employs more than 3,000 academic and administrative staff. The university is particularly renowned for its contributions to mathematics, physics, and engineering, and maintains strong ties with national research institutions such as the National Research Council (CNR). A unique aspect of the University of Pisa is its close integration with cutting-edge research facilities, offering students direct access to advanced laboratories and collaborative projects.
Situated in the Tuscany region, the city of Pisa offers students a vibrant yet manageable environment, with a rich historical backdrop and easy access to both coastal and inland destinations. The city is well-connected by public transport and lies just an hour from Florence, making it an ideal base for exploring central Italy. Pisa’s compact size and student-centered atmosphere make it easy to navigate and settle into daily life. A fun fact: while the Leaning Tower is its most famous landmark, Pisa is also home to one of world's oldest botanical gardens, founded in 1544, which continues to serve as a research and teaching facility for the university.

Description
The University of Salzburg (Paris Lodron University), founded in 1622 and re-established in 1962, is the largest institution of higher education in the province of Salzburg. With approximately 18,000 students and 2,900 staff members across six faculties and 34 departments, it offers a diverse and interdisciplinary academic environment. Exchange students are welcomed into a vibrant international community and can choose from around 90 degree programmes, many of which include English-taught courses. The university’s central location in the historic and culturally rich city of Salzburg—combined with its modern facilities and strong research orientation—makes it an ideal destination for students seeking both academic excellence and an inspiring living experience in the heart of Europe.
Salzburg is one of the safest cities in the world, and the University’s excellent student-to-staff ratio provides students with a supportive environment and a wonderful learning culture. The University’s twenty sites are located in and around Salzburg’s historical center, which is a UNESCO listed world heritage site. Situated in the heart of Europe, Salzburg offers fantastic connections to neighbouring countries and to some of Austria’s most beautiful alpine national parks. It is also a culturally vibrant city, home to world-famous performing arts and music festivals. Operating in this setting, the University of Salzburg offers international students and scholars abroad a unique range of experiences.

Description
The University of Zurich (UZH), founded in 1833, is the largest university in Switzerland, enrolling around 28,000 students and supported by roughly 3,700 full‑time faculty. It offers the broadest range of subjects of any Swiss higher education institution, with seven faculties covering medicine, law, economics, natural sciences, humanities, theology, and veterinary medicine. UZH is internationally recognized for cutting‑edge research in life sciences, biomedical studies, climate science, and social sciences - featuring leading institutes such as the Institute of Molecular Life Sciences - and encourages a strong interdisciplinary approach. About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

Description
Established in 1477, Uppsala University is the oldest and prestigious university in Sweden and the Nordic region. Located in Uppsala, it offers a wide range of academic disciplines across its nine faculties, including the renowned Faculty of Law. With a strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, the Faculty of Law provides a vibrant research environment and highly regarded legal education. The unique training model and active involvement of prominent researchers ensure high-quality education and enriching research experiences for students. The university's historic legacy and commitment to excellence make it a sought-after destination for students and scholars alike.
With about 52,000 students in a compact city of just over 230,000 residents, Uppsala has a uniquely youthful atmosphere where bikes outnumber cars and student life shapes much of the local culture. The city is known for its beautiful river views, historic university buildings, and cozy cafés that fill with students studying or socializing. A big part of life here revolves around the traditional student “nations”—13 social clubs that organize everything from formal dinners and choirs to club nights and themed parties, making it easy to meet people and dive into Swedish traditions. Despite being a historic city, Uppsala feels dynamic and international, offering plenty of cultural events, outdoor activities, and excellent train connections to Stockholm just 40 minutes away.

Language courses may constitute a maximum of seven credits. Language instruction: 1 credit corresponds to 1 class (= 90 minutes) for 15 weeks = 22.5 hours of teaching. You should have completed two years of study (120 ECTS) before enrolling at ETH.
Please note that some courses are restricted, e.g., in the number of participants and that you cannot register for any courses ending with –AAL.
2/3 of your course credits must be offered by the department you are applying to. 1/3 of your credits can be taken from other departments and / or the University of Zurich. 
Students are free to combine Bachelor and Master courses, independent of their level of studies at the home university, provided they have the necessary prerequisites to follow the courses. There is not limit as to the amount of MSc courses Bachelor students can take.
You will be studying at The Department of Environmental Systems Science (D-USYS). The departmet offers programmes in both Environmental and Agricultural Sciences.
At D-USYS, advanced knowledge in natural sciences (biology, chemistry, physics) and mathematics are highly recommended. The language of instruction is mainly German for the Bachelor and mainly English for the Master. Information about the language of instruction, prerequisites as well as possible restrictions for each course is listed in the Course Catalogue.
If you want to do a research project or write you bachelor’s thesis at ETH, you must enclose written confirmation from your supervisor at ETH Zurich in your application. Project work is expected to be full-time. A combination of course work and a project must be discussed with the supervisor.
It is not possible to write your thesis project as an exchange student at ETH Zurich.
