University of Leuven
Description
KU Leuven is one of Europe’s leading research universities, consistently ranked among the top institutions worldwide for innovation and academic excellence. Founded in 1425, it is the oldest university in Belgium and currently hosts over 64,000 students, including more than 15,000 international students from over 140 countries. With 15 faculties offering nearly 230 degree programmes, KU Leuven provides a comprehensive and interdisciplinary academic environment. The university is renowned for its cutting-edge research, with over 10,000 peer-reviewed publications annually and 168 ERC grants awarded since 2007. Exchange students benefit from a wide selection of English-taught courses, strong academic support, and access to world-class facilities across its campuses.
Located in the historic city of Leuven, just 25 km east of Brussels, KU Leuven offers students a unique blend of academic tradition and modern urban life. Leuven is a vibrant university town with a youthful atmosphere, shaped by its large student population and rich cultural heritage. The city is known for its medieval architecture, lively cafés, and green spaces, making it both welcoming and walkable. Its central location in Europe provides excellent connectivity to major cities such as Brussels, Amsterdam, Paris, and Cologne. For students seeking a stimulating academic experience in a dynamic and international setting, KU Leuven and the city of Leuven together offer an ideal destination for an enriching exchange semester.

Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam
Description
The Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU or VU Amsterdam) is a public research university in Amsterdam, Netherlands, founded in 1880. The VU is one of two large, publicly funded research universities in the city, the other being the University of Amsterdam (UvA). The translation of the Dutch name Vrije Universiteit is "Free University". "Free" refers to independence of the university from both the State and the Dutch Reformed Church. Both within and outside the university, the institution is commonly referred to as "the VU".
VU Amsterdam is a unique university with faculties in the humanities, STEM, social sciences and medical sciences. Our education and research are closely linked. We combine our top position in research with a strong social orientation, which has resulted in an excellent international reputation. Our education and research are distinctly multidisciplinary. This is reinforced by the fact that our faculties are all located on one campus. Description
The Universidad Complutense de Madrid (UCM), founded in 1293, is one of the oldest and most prestigious universities in Spain and Europe. With over 86,000 students and a wide range of academic disciplines, it is also one of the largest traditional (non-distance) universities in Europe. UCM is a public research university with a strong emphasis on academic excellence, social responsibility, and international collaboration. Its two main campuses—Moncloa and Somosaguas—are located in Madrid and offer students access to extensive libraries, research centers, and cultural facilities. The university has a long history of academic innovation and was among the first in the world to award doctoral degrees to women.
Madrid, the capital of Spain, offers a dynamic and student-friendly environment with a rich cultural heritage and excellent infrastructure. The city’s extensive public transport system—including metro, buses, and commuter trains—makes it easy for students to navigate between campuses and explore the city. Madrid is known for its world-class museums, lively neighborhoods, and green spaces like Retiro Park. A fun fact: Madrid is one of the highest capital cities in Europe, sitting at 667 meters above sea level, and its name is believed to come from the Arabic word Magerit, meaning “place of abundant water.” This blend of history, accessibility, and vibrant city life makes it an ideal destination for students.

Description
École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales (EHESS) is a distinguished graduate-only research institution, founded in 1984 as a grand établissement alongside the Collège de France and Sciences Po. With its main campus in Paris and additional sites in Marseille, Lyon, and Toulouse, EHESS is internationally recognized for its interdisciplinary approach to the social sciences and humanities. The university has been home to some of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, including Pierre Bourdieu, Claude Lévi-Strauss, and Jacques Derrida. Today, EHESS hosts a diverse student body of approximately 3,000 students from around the world, drawn by its unique academic environment that combines rigorous historical analysis with contemporary research.
Situated in central Paris, EHESS offers students an exceptional opportunity to study in one of Europe’s foremost intellectual and cultural capitals. The university’s location places students at the heart of a city renowned for its libraries, archives, museums, and vibrant academic life. Paris provides a stimulating backdrop for scholarly engagement, with countless opportunities to attend public lectures, exhibitions, and cultural events. For students seeking a rich academic experience alongside immersion in a dynamic urban setting, EHESS represents an ideal destination for an exchange semester.

Description
Leiden University, founded in 1575, is the oldest university in the Netherlands and one of Europe’s leading research institutions. It serves nearly 34,000 students from over 120 nationalities and employs more than 6,400 staff members. The university is spread across two cities—Leiden and The Hague—and is home to 32 research institutes that focus on interdisciplinary collaboration across fields such as law, life sciences, artificial intelligence, and global governance. With a strong emphasis on academic freedom and integrity, Leiden University integrates research and teaching, encouraging students to engage with complex societal challenges in a global context.
The city of Leiden offers a rich and student-friendly environment, combining historical charm with modern amenities. Known for its picturesque canals, vibrant cultural scene, and compact layout, Leiden is easy to navigate—most places are just a short bike ride away. The city hosts numerous festivals, museums, and events throughout the year, and is well-connected to other Dutch cities like Amsterdam and The Hague. A fun fact: Leiden is the birthplace of Rembrandt van Rijn and also home to the world’s oldest university observatory, established in 1633—making it a city where art and science have long flourished side by side.

Panteion University of Social and Political Sciences, founded in 1927, is one of Greece’s oldest and most respected institutions dedicated exclusively to the social sciences. University of Coimbra
Description
The University of Coimbra, founded in 1290 by King Dinis, is the oldest continuously operating university in Portugal and one of the oldest in the world. Relocated permanently to Coimbra in 1537, it occupies the historic Royal Palace, now known as the Paço das Escolas, and has been recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2013. The university enrolls over 25,000 students, with around 20 percent coming from abroad, and is organized into eight faculties offering a wide range of bachelor’s, master’s, and doctoral programs. Blending centuries of tradition with modern academic excellence, it is especially renowned in fields such as medicine, engineering, social sciences, and the humanities. The campus is home to remarkable landmarks like the Baroque Biblioteca Joanina, which houses tens of thousands of historic volumes, and a botanical garden dating back to 1772.
Living in Coimbra as a student means becoming part of a lively city where academic life and local culture are closely intertwined. The medieval old town and university buildings create a unique atmosphere, deeply shaped by traditions like the student capes and annual ceremonies such as Queima das Fitas and Latada. Students find community through the Associação Académica de Coimbra, the oldest student union in Portugal, which organizes a wide variety of cultural and sports activities, from theater and music to rugby, chess, and rowing. With its scenic riverside location along the Mondego, rich academic heritage, and vibrant student life, Coimbra offers an immersive and memorable university experience.

About the agreement between the University of Copenhagen (UCPH) and the University of Edinburgh (UoE): 
1 university-wide agreement, covering subject areas within Arts, Humanities, Social Sciences and Science, plus:
University of Edinburgh – university-wide
6 subject specific agreements in the following high-pressure subject areas at UoE: Law, English, Sociology, Anthropology, Psychology, and Political Science.

University of Edinburgh – Psychology

University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – especially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement.
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

Description
Tokai University is a private university established by Dr. Shigeyoshi Matsumae in1942. "Tokai" of "Tokai University" refers to the sea to the east of Asia, namely the Pacific Ocean, and symbolizes the desire to foster a rich spirit and broad perspectives. Tokai University consists of 7 campuses in Sapporo, Takanawa, Yoyogi, Shonan, Isehara, Shimizu and Kumamoto, which are comprised of 19 schools and colleges made up of 75 departments, majors, and programs. Its graduate schools offer 17 programs. The total student population is approximately 30,000. The founding spirit and objectives of Tokai University are to contribute to human society, to foster active, talented people and to arouse interest in our earth and a sense of responsibility for the future of mankind. Tokai University's educational guidelines ground these objectives by offering a sound and reliable way of thinking as a basis for living and cultivating the richness of humanity. Tokai University maintains academic exchange agreements with more than 120 universities/organizations in approx. 40 countries. A wide range of international exchange activities are being actively pursued, from those on the academic front involving students, researchers, joint-research projects, and the exchange of academic resources, to cultural and athletic exchanges. There are about 960 international students on campus. Tokai University has five offices abroad: Denmark, Austria, USA (junior college in Hawaii), Korea and Thailand. Postgraduate: You can enrol in both undergraduate (Levels 1–4) and postgraduate (Level 5) subjects, as long as you meet any subject prerequisites.You must however enrol in at least one Level 5 subject

You should avoid selecting subjects that run before the start of your chosen study period as this could create visa issues i.e. a block mode subject or summer course. You can only choose subject in the study period model.

JCU offers a wide range of field trips. Please note that some field trips incur additional costs. More information is available through the relevant College.

Please note that the subjects below are not available to Study Abroad/Exchange students:
- Honours subjects and Postgraduate level (Level 4 and 5) (Undergraduate only)
- Any MD, DS, ML, PC, PS, RH, TV subjects

Description
The University of Crete, established in 1973, is a public, research-oriented institution with campuses in Rethymno and Heraklion. It serves a student body of approximately 20,000 undergraduates and 2,500 postgraduates, supported by over 500 academic staff and 420 administrative personnel. The university is organized into five schools and sixteen departments, offering a wide range of academic disciplines. It is known for its strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, participating actively in European programs such as Erasmus and maintaining partnerships with institutions across Europe and the United States. A unique feature of the University of Crete is its close integration with regional research centers, including the Foundation for Research and Technology – Hellas (FORTH), which enhances opportunities for students to engage in high-level scientific work.
Crete, the largest and most populous of the Greek islands, offers a rich cultural and natural environment for students. The island combines ancient history, Mediterranean landscapes, and a vibrant local culture shaped by diverse influences from Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Students can explore archaeological sites, hike through dramatic gorges, and enjoy local cuisine rooted in the renowned Cretan diet. Public transport connects the university campuses with nearby towns and beaches, making it easy to experience the island’s varied offerings. A fun fact: Crete is home to two of the world’s few pink sand beaches, Balos and Elafonissi, adding a unique natural attraction to the student experience.

Description
UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

The modules with the numerical digits 1000 - 2000 are for bachelor's degree students. The modules with the numerical digits 2000 - 300 are for master's students - it means you must have completed your bachelor's degree or 180 ECTS at your home university to be able to choose these modules.

Enrollment at UiT offers the possibility to study at University Center in Svalbard (UNIS) – however, you will need to apply to both UiT, AND you must apply directly to UNIS. You must follow both application procedures in detail and both UiT and UNIS require that you attach certified copies of official transcripts to document your grades and student status.

UiT as a back-up plan:
If you are interested in studying at UNIS, you should be aware that UNIS usually has more applicants than places for exchange students and every semester there are students who are not offered admission. It is therefore recommended that you consider applying for courses at UiT as well as UNIS. That way you will have the option to attend UiT as a second choice if your application is rejected at UNIS.

Description
The University of Bern, founded in 1834, is one of Switzerland’s leading comprehensive universities, enrolling around 19,000 students across eight faculties and offering a wide spectrum of programs in the humanities, natural sciences, law, economics, medicine, and social sciences. With a strong focus on interdisciplinary research, sustainability, and global cooperation, the university ranks among the top 120–200 universities worldwide, depending on the field. It is particularly renowned for cutting-edge research in climate science, space, and health. Students benefit from close links to governmental institutions and international organizations located in Bern, providing unique opportunities for internships, networking, and real-world projects.
Living in Bern as a student means enjoying all the advantages of Switzerland’s picturesque capital city. With its UNESCO-listed medieval Old Town, lively café culture, and riverside spots along the Aare for swimming or relaxing, Bern combines a laid-back atmosphere with rich cultural offerings. The university’s facilities are mostly concentrated in the welcoming Länggasse district, where student life thrives thanks to numerous associations, events, and support services like the Welcome Center and student union. Excellent public transport, bike-friendly streets, and proximity to the Alps make it easy to explore both the city and Switzerland’s stunning natural landscapes, offering an outstanding quality of life alongside your studies.

University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – specially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement.
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

The Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU or VU Amsterdam) is a public research university in Amsterdam, Netherlands, founded in 1880. You are expected to take at least 60% of your courses at TUM Informatics. If you wish to attend courses at other departments, be aware of prerequisites and matching timetables.

Bachelor students with at least 2 years (120 ECTS) of prior studies in Computer Science, Computer Engineering or a similar subject can take courses at Master level. This means (in order to take English taught courses) you need to meet the following minimum requirements: Programming, Algorithms, Data Structures, Mathematics, Software Engineering. Preferably: Data Bases, Computer Architecture, Operating Systems, Networks, Automata, Formal Languages

Please note that IN5xxx, IN8xxx and xxHNxxxx modules are not open for exchange students.

In case you want to pursue a thesis during your exchange stay at TUM Informatics, you have to follow the regular TUM rules for thesis on bachelor's and master's level. You are expected to stay for a year if you wish pursue your thesis in the second semester
  At IN.TUM a Bachelor’s Thesis has usually 15 ECTS (duration of four months) or 25 ECTS (duration of 5 months). A Master’s Thesis lasts 6 months and has 30 ECTS. A thesis has to be registered and submitted in time and according to our rules. 

You must comply with scheduled exam dates and times which usually to take place between February and March (winter semester) and between July and September (summer semester).
  Resits/Retakes at TUM Informatics are offered for some but not all lectures. Resits take place in late September/October (registration in September) for summer semester, and late March/April for winter semester (registration in March). Students enrolled at TUM during the current semester can take the resits of the upcoming retake-period.

Description
Athens University of Economics and Business (AUEB), founded in 1920, is Greece’s leading institution in the fields of economics, business, and information sciences. With eight academic departments spanning economics, international and European studies, business administration, marketing, accounting, informatics, and statistics, AUEB offers a strong academic foundation rooted in both theory and applied research. The university also hosts a variety of postgraduate programs, many of which are taught in English, and it maintains active participation in European exchange programs like Erasmus+. Known for its international outlook, research excellence, and highly qualified faculty, AUEB provides a rigorous and relevant education, especially for students interested in global business and economic issues.
Located in the heart of Athens, the university gives students easy access to a lively and culturally rich city. From ancient landmarks and bustling markets to cafés and local neighborhoods, Athens blends history and modern energy in a way few European cities can. Exchange students benefit from being part of a dynamic urban environment, with opportunities to explore the city’s vibrant culture while studying in a well-connected central location. The combination of strong academics and an exciting city life makes AUEB an appealing choice for students looking to deepen their knowledge of economics and business while experiencing life in the Greek capital.

With an efficient public transport system and a welcoming atmosphere, Bielefeld is an ideal setting for exchange students looking for a mix of academic excellence and everyday livability.

Ca’ Foscari University of Venice, established in 1868 as Italy’s first business school, has evolved into a comprehensive institution offering a wide array of academic programs. Exchange students have access to a diverse selection of courses, including subjects in international business, cultural studies, environmental science, and linguistics, many of which are offered in English. Ca’ Foscari maintains a strong emphasis on global engagement and intercultural exchange, providing international students with academic experiences deeply connected to the university’s historical strengths in commerce, languages, and cultural heritage.
Situated in the heart of Venice, Ca’ Foscari’s campuses are embedded within the city’s iconic canals and historic architecture. The unique urban landscape offers students a learning environment that blends academic life with the artistic, cultural, and maritime traditions of Venice. From exploring local markets and museums to participating in university-organized cultural events, exchange students experience daily life in a city renowned for its beauty, history, and vibrant student atmosphere. The University also offers Italian language courses for international students and a ‘buddy’ program with local students.

Restrictions
Course switches are not possible after the course registration deadline(s). Reason for this is that Maastricht University uses the Problem-Based Learning system. A lot of planning goes into finding enough rooms and teachers/tutors, and to make sure each tutorial group is about the same size (average group size is 15 students).
Course information
In the SBE online exchange course database, you can find all information you need about SBE’s bachelor’s and master’s courses, such as the course coordinator(s), learning outcomes and competences, the number of ECTS credits, literature, and any prerequisites.
  At Maastricht they offer Problem-Based Learning (PBL) with a different, more interactive way of learning compared to traditional university education. You will not have to spend hours listening to lectures, although there are some, usually at the start of the study period. Instead, per course you will attend a two-hour tutorial group meeting, twice a week. These meetings form the core part of the study programme and are the driving force for learning. Within the tutorial groups (with 8 to 15 students), problems are presented and researched as cases.

Description
Tohoku University, founded in 1907, is one of Japan’s top national research institutions and has a long-standing reputation for academic excellence and innovation. Exchange students can choose from a wide range of courses taught in English across fields such as materials science, engineering, political science, and the humanities. The university also offers opportunities in business-related subjects through its School of Economics and Management, where students can explore topics like finance, international business, and marketing from both theoretical and applied perspectives. Tohoku is known for its research-first philosophy and interdisciplinary approach, and students benefit from small class sizes and close interaction with faculty.
Located in Sendai, a lively city often called Japan’s “City of Trees,” Tohoku University offers an inviting academic environment within a vibrant, student-friendly setting. The city is surrounded by mountains and coastline, making it easy to enjoy nature year-round, while still offering the convenience and culture of a modern urban center. Parks, cafés, art spaces, and festivals help create an engaging atmosphere, and student clubs across academic and cultural interests provide an excellent way for exchange students to connect with peers and get involved in university life.

Université Toulouse Capitole is a prominent French public university specializing in law, economics, management, and political science. With roots dating back to the 13th century, the university has evolved into a modern institution known for its academic rigor and international outlook. It hosts several renowned research centers and offers a wide range of graduate and postgraduate programs, many of which are taught in English. Its commitment to interdisciplinary research and professional development makes it an excellent choice for students seeking both academic depth and career-oriented training.

Toulouse is known for its vibrant cultural life, historical charm, and strong presence in the aerospace and technology sectors. The city combines a rich architectural heritage with a youthful energy, making it an ideal environment for both academic focus and social engagement. Students can enjoy a wide range of cultural events, outdoor activities, and a welcoming atmosphere that supports both learning and personal growth. An exchange semester at Toulouse Capitole promises not only academic enrichment but also a memorable experience in one of southern France’s most attractive urban settings.

Description
Universidade NOVA de Lisboa, founded in 1973, is a dynamic public university recognized for its innovative spirit and strong international profile. NOVA has grown into one of Portugal’s leading institutions, with around 20,000 students enrolled across nine academic units that cover disciplines ranging from social sciences, humanities, law, and business to science, technology, medicine, and public health. Its schools and faculties consistently rank among the best in Portugal and Europe, known for combining high-quality teaching with research that addresses contemporary challenges. NOVA’s commitment to creating and sharing scientific, cultural, and artistic knowledge, along with training that is deeply anchored in research, reflects its mission to contribute actively to the social and economic development of the communities it serves.
Studying in Lisbon offers students the chance to live in a vibrant European capital known for its mild climate, historical neighborhoods, and rich cultural life. NOVA’s various campuses are well integrated into the city, providing modern facilities and fostering an environment that encourages collaboration, creativity, and global perspectives. With Lisbon’s welcoming atmosphere and excellent quality of life, students at NOVA enjoy both rigorous academic experiences and the benefits of living in a lively, cosmopolitan setting.

Description
Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne is one of France’s leading public universities, with a strong academic tradition rooted in the historic Sorbonne. It serves over 40,000 students and employs more than 1,400 academic and administrative staff. The university is known for its focus on humanities, social sciences, law, and economics, and maintains active research centres across these fields. It plays a central role in French academic life and contributes to international research networks. Its structure supports a broad academic offering while maintaining close links to national institutions and cultural organisations, making it a distinctive institution within the French higher education system.
The university is located in central Paris, with sites spread across several historic buildings in the Latin Quarter and other parts of the city. This location places students in direct contact with the intellectual and cultural life of the capital, with access to libraries, archives, museums, and public institutions. Paris offers a wide range of academic and cultural resources, as well as efficient transport connections to other regions and countries. For students considering an exchange semester, Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne provides an opportunity to study in a city closely tied to European academic and cultural history.

Description
The University of Amsterdam (UvA) is one of Europe’s top research universities, known for its academic excellence, broad disciplinary offerings, and strong international orientation. Founded in 1632, UvA offers a wide range of programs across seven faculties, including humanities, social and behavioral sciences, economics and business, law, and science. With over 40,000 students, including a large international cohort, the university fosters a vibrant and diverse academic community. UvA is particularly recognized for its research-led teaching, interdisciplinary collaboration, and commitment to addressing global challenges through innovative scholarship.
Located in the heart of Amsterdam, the university offers students the opportunity to study in one of the most culturally rich and globally connected cities in Europe. Amsterdam is known for its historic canals, world-class museums, and dynamic urban life. The city’s compact layout, excellent public transport, and welcoming atmosphere make it ideal for student living. Whether exploring the city’s artistic heritage, engaging in public debates, or enjoying its lively cafés and green spaces, exchange students at UvA will find themselves in an inspiring environment that supports both academic growth and personal discovery.

➢ Official certificate attesting adequate knowledge of Catalan or Spanish with a minimum level of B1. A language test like DELE og CELU with a minimum language level B1 will be acceptable.
  For courses taught in English, exchange students must submit:
➢ Official certificate attesting adequate knowledge of English with a min. level of B2.
  However, after the nomination process, you will be able to retrieve a language confirmation from UCPH in Mobility Online certifying that you have an English language level equivalent to B2 on the European CEFR.  As a UCPH student you fullfill the requirements of an English level of B2 from your previous studies before your enrolment at UCPH.
Study load
30 ECTS per semester.
Restrictions
Exchange students have access to all the bachelor course offer of the Faculty, as specified on the course catalogue. However, courses have limited space and it is important students coming on exchange at our institution can be flexible regarding the courses they intend to do during their exchange, as we cannot guarantee enrolment into specific courses. In this sense, we strongly suggest exchange students complete all compulsory modules required for graduation at home.

See info on course offers - scroll down to see course descriptions.

Description
The University of Porto, established in 1911, is one of Portugal’s top public research universities and the country’s second largest by enrollment. It serves around 28,000 students, including approximately 11,000 postgraduate students, across 13 faculties, a business school, and a range of specialized institutes, supported by more than 2,300 academic staff. The university offers a wide selection of programs in fields such as economics, management, engineering, law, humanities, science, and the arts, with over 60 bachelor’s degrees, and more than 160 master’s programs. U.Porto’s mission is to create scientific, cultural, and artistic knowledge, to offer advanced education strongly anchored in research, to drive the social and economic value of knowledge, and to actively contribute to the progress of the communities it serves. Its School of Economics and Management is particularly well regarded, fostering close connections with businesses and international institutions and preparing graduates for competitive global careers.
Living in Porto as a student means enjoying a vibrant, welcoming city known for its rich culture, historic charm, and affordable lifestyle. The cost of living is relatively low compared to many other Western European cities, with budget-friendly options for food, housing, and public transportation. Porto’s extensive metro, bus, and train networks make it easy to explore, while the atmospheric old town, riverside cafés, music venues, and art spaces add to the lively student experience. University associations organize a wide variety of cultural, sports, and volunteer activities that help students quickly feel part of the community. With friendly locals and easy access to Portugal’s coastline and beautiful northern landscapes, studying in Porto offers a dynamic and fulfilling university life.

Description
The University of Zurich (UZH), founded in 1833, is the largest university in Switzerland, enrolling around 28,000 students and supported by roughly 3,700 full‑time faculty. It offers the broadest range of subjects of any Swiss higher education institution, with seven faculties covering medicine, law, economics, natural sciences, humanities, theology, and veterinary medicine. UZH is internationally recognized for cutting‑edge research in life sciences, biomedical studies, climate science, and social sciences - featuring leading institutes such as the Institute of Molecular Life Sciences - and encourages a strong interdisciplinary approach. About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – specially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement.
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

Students have access to a variety of sports at affordable rates, both within the center's events and in affiliated sports facilities. Moreover, the university has established a network of relationships and signed agreements with cultural institutions and associations in Milan and other cities. This allows for vibrant cultural exchanges between the academic and local communities, providing students with discounts at museums, theaters, cinemas, and more, further enhancing their educational experience. Through its commitment to research, education, and the holistic development of its students, the University of Milan remains at the forefront of academic excellence and actively contributes to the intellectual, cultural, and social fabric of both Milan and the wider academic community.

Students are free to combine Bachelor and Master courses, independent of their level of studies at the home university, provided they have the necessary prerequisites to follow the courses. There is not limit as to the amount of MSc courses Bachelor students can take.
You will be studying at The Department of Earth and Climate Sciences (D-EAPS). At D-EAPS, you can choose courses from all levels of courses in the Earth Sciences programme. In doing so, 2/3 of the ECTS credits should be earned in courses of D-EAPS (course numbers beginning with 651-...) or, if applicable, in courses of D-USYS (701-...) by arrangement.
If you want to do a research project or write you bachelor’s thesis at ETH, you must enclose written confirmation from your supervisor at ETH Zurich in your application. Project work is expected to be full-time. A combination of course work and a project must be discussed with the supervisor.
It is not possible to write your thesis project as an exchange student at ETH Zurich.

Located in the vibrant capital city of Warsaw, the university benefits from its dynamic environment, which serves as a center of political, economic, and cultural activity. Warsaw's thriving social scene and rich cultural offerings make it an attractive destination for students seeking a comprehensive educational experience.
Comprising 21 faculties and an impressive 126 buildings, the University of Warsaw covers an extensive range of academic disciplines, including applied linguistics, social sciences, economics, humanities, and natural sciences. With a student population of approximately 40,000, the university fosters an environment that encourages intellectual curiosity, critical thinking, and academic excellence. Yearly appraisals conducted by the university affirm the quality of education, as a majority of students express satisfaction with their choice of institution and academic programs. The University of Warsaw continues to uphold its commitment to delivering world-class education and nurturing the intellectual growth and success of its students.

Humboldt-Universität zu Berlin
Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

University of Salerno
Description
The University of Salerno, with origins dating back to the medieval Schola Medica Salernitana, was formally re-established in 1944 and has since grown into a modern academic institution with over 35,000 students. Located in the town of Fisciano, the university operates a large, integrated campus that brings together teaching, research, and student life in one location. It is particularly recognized for its investment in digital infrastructure and sustainability, offering students access to advanced facilities and a well-organized academic environment. Unlike many Italian universities, the University of Salerno is structured as a campus university, which fosters a strong sense of community and makes student services easily accessible.
The university is situated in the Campania region, just a short distance from the Amalfi Coast and the city of Salerno. This location offers students a mix of natural beauty, historical sites, and modern urban life. Salerno itself is a lively coastal city with a walkable historic center, a scenic promenade, and regular cultural events. The region is well-connected by public transport, making it easy to explore nearby destinations such as Naples, Pompeii, and Paestum.

Description
The University of Turin, founded in 1404, is one of Italy’s largest and most respected public universities, with a student population of over 80,000 and more than 4,000 academic and administrative staff. It offers a broad academic portfolio across 27 departments and maintains a strong reputation in both humanities and sciences. The university is known for its commitment to research excellence and internationalization, with numerous partnerships and exchange agreements worldwide. A notable feature is its integration with local institutions and industries, which provides students with opportunities for practical experience and interdisciplinary collaboration within the city and region.
Turin, located in the Piedmont region of northwestern Italy, is a dynamic city with a rich cultural and industrial heritage. It offers students a mix of historical architecture, contemporary art, and green spaces, all within a well-connected urban setting. The city is known for its cafés, museums, and proximity to the Alps, making it attractive for both academic and leisure activities. Public transport is efficient, and the cost of living is generally lower than in other major Italian cities.

UiT The Arctic University of Norway
Description
UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

Restrictions
As a law exchange student you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad.
  The majority of courses are offered as lectures called ‘cours magistraux’ (CM) and generally they have a duration of three (3) hours. Educational material such as text books are not used at lectures, instead students must take notes from the lecturers' presentations and use these when preparing for the final exams.

In addition to ordinary lectures you can register for 1 class called ‘Travaux Dirigés’ (TD) – based on a special permission for Danish law students. With registration in an TD class which is connected to one of the ordinary law courses with lectures – you can gain additional ECTS. The education in a TD class will be interactive with presentations, assignments, group work etc. – an educational style familiar to the teaching methods in Copenhagen.

You are strongly advised to read travel reports in KUnet and student evaluations in Mobility online from Aix-Marseille Université as the teaching style will be significantly different from what you are used to at UCPH.

Restrictions
Law students must follow a full study load of min. 12 credits which corresponds to 30 ECTS per semester. Be aware that credit requirements may be different for other exchange students at Duke Law School.
  Observe that attendance in classes is mandatory - very little absence may be permitted approx. max 1-2 times!!
Course information
Generally, Duke Law School accepts very few exchange students per semester less than 10 exchange students will not be unusual; exchange students at Duke join approx. 100 international students in the LLM program (who are staying for a whole year program). Exchange students combined with LLM students are then enrolled in courses with American JD students. All students are graded on the same scale. 
  The style of teaching is based on the socratic method, meaning students are actively involved by use of cold calling. The professors expect you to be well prepared for classes, so that you will be ready to answer questions or participate in class discussions, if you get called upon in class. Faculty members will make note of students with excessive absences and may refuse to allow such students to sit for exams.

Some courses are not available to LLM or exchange students. Exchange students should therefore have alternative courses selected and approved by their home university in case their first choices are not available. Exchange administrators are encouraged to check with the International Studies department in advance with questions about course content and appropriateness of particular courses for exchange students.

Housing
Duke University does not provide on-campus housing. Housing suggestions are, however, made available to students via the Admitted Students Website, for which login and password information is issued after an admission offer is accepted.

Apply for housing as soon as possible as it may be difficult to find housing for just one semester compared to LLM students who are renting for a whole year. May also be challenging with the local transportation offers, see reference to former student reports on this page.

Though tuition is waived between the partner universities, students are responsible for educational fees. Bills are issued and normally due before students arrive on campus to begin their program. See info on the estimated fees below:

• Healt Insurance $1,688
• Law Student Activity Fee - $75
• Graduate Student Activity Fee $20
• Graduate Student Services Fee - $12
• Transcript Fee - $120

Health Insurance
Students with F1 or J1 visas are automatically enrolled in the Duke SMIP (Student Medical Insurance Plan). See additional info on enrollment or waiver proces.
  In addition to a possible mandatory insurance at your host university, UCPH recommends that Danish students on an exchange programme take out an insurance coverage with a Danish insurance company to make sure that you are sufficiently covered especially during your activities and trips outside the campus area of your host university and in case an urgent situation should occur with need of home transportation for DK.

Description
Eötvös Loránd University (ELTE), established in 1635, is Hungary's oldest and largest university, renowned for its academic excellence and research contributions. Situated in Budapest, ELTE offers a diverse range of programs across various disciplines, including humanities, social sciences, natural sciences, and law. The university provides courses in English, making it accessible to international students. With a commitment to fostering a dynamic learning environment, ELTE encourages critical thinking, interdisciplinary collaboration, and global perspectives. Students have the opportunity to engage in cutting-edge research and benefit from the expertise of distinguished faculty members.
Located in the heart of Budapest, ELTE's campuses offer a blend of historical charm and modern facilities. The city, known for its rich cultural heritage and vibrant atmosphere, provides students with a unique living and learning experience. Budapest boasts a wide array of cultural attractions, including museums, theaters, and historic landmarks, as well as a lively student community. The city's efficient public transportation system ensures easy access to various parts of Budapest, allowing students to explore and immerse themselves in the local culture. Studying at ELTE offers not only academic growth but also the chance to experience life in one of Europe's most captivating cities.

Erasmus University Rotterdam is a leading international research university known for its strong focus on societal impact and academic excellence. Whether exploring contemporary art museums, attending music festivals, or enjoying the city’s waterfront, exchange students will find Rotterdam to be a lively and inspiring place to complement their academic journey at Erasmus University.

Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

Accepted proof of language proficiency (required AFTER nomination)
Be aware that 95% of all law courses/modules are taught in German. In addition, information about timetables, exams, registration processes will only be available in German.

Humboldt Universität requires that all incoming exchange students take an online German language test (called the C-Test). After the application round in Cph there will be an application process for Humboldt where this test is taken online with texts to be filled in and parts of reading, understanding, and giving summaries; the C-test is all in writing, no oral part.

If your result is less than 50 points, you must apply for an intensive course in German at the Humboldt Universität (HU) language center (Sprachenzentrum) and send the confirmation of this application to HU. Preconditions for the admission will be the passed test (equivalent to level B2 with 50 points) or the intensive course.
The language centre of Humboldt Universität offers a number of language courses for international students as well as intensive courses before the beginning of each semester (or during the semester).

30 ECTS per semester
Study load for graduate students
30 ECTS per semester
Restrictions
As a law exchange student, you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad.

SWS-indication – transferred to ECTS value:
All Law classes taught in German have an “SWS” indication showing the number of class hours per week. Courses with 2 SWS = 5 ECTS, 4 SWS = 10 ECTS and 6 SWS = 15 ECTS.
  In some course descriptions you may find LP-values which will also correspond to course ECTS-values.

The law programme at Humboldt Universität is not divided into a bachelor and master degree. Therefore, the whole range of elective courses are offered to both so-called 'bachelor' and 'master' students. If the catalogue for the upcoming semester is not online yet, please use the one from the year before, since the courses are, in general, the same.
  The style of teaching at Humboldt Universität may be characterized as a bit old school with a teacher giving lectures and the students listening and taking notes. Generally, there is very little group work or group presentations, i.e. less active student involvement.

Description
Leiden University, founded in 1575, is the oldest university in the Netherlands and one of Europe’s leading research institutions. It serves nearly 34,000 students from over 120 nationalities and employs more than 6,400 staff members. The university is spread across two cities—Leiden and The Hague—and is home to 32 research institutes that focus on interdisciplinary collaboration across fields such as law, life sciences, artificial intelligence, and global governance. With a strong emphasis on academic freedom and integrity, Leiden University integrates research and teaching, encouraging students to engage with complex societal challenges in a global context.
The city of Leiden offers a rich and student-friendly environment, combining historical charm with modern amenities. Known for its picturesque canals, vibrant cultural scene, and compact layout, Leiden is easy to navigate—most places are just a short bike ride away. The city hosts numerous festivals, museums, and events throughout the year, and is well-connected to other Dutch cities like Amsterdam and The Hague. A fun fact: Leiden is the birthplace of Rembrandt van Rijn and also home to the world’s oldest university observatory, established in 1633—making it a city where art and science have long flourished side by side.

Description
Luiss University, officially Libera Università Internazionale degli Studi Sociali Guido Carli, is a renowned private university in Rome specializing in the social sciences. Established in 1974, Luiss offers academic programs across four main departments: Economics and Finance, Business and Management, Law, and Political Science. The university emphasizes a practical and interdisciplinary approach to learning, closely connected with Italy’s business community and public institutions. Exchange students have access to a broad selection of English-taught courses, with opportunities to engage in case studies, project work, and interactive seminars designed to mirror real-world challenges. Located in Rome’s elegant Parioli and Trieste neighborhoods, Luiss University provides a campus environment within one of Europe’s most historic and dynamic cities. Students can easily explore Rome’s rich cultural life, with museums, galleries, markets, and iconic landmarks just a short distance from campus. The university fosters a vibrant student community through various academic and cultural events, allowing exchange students to immerse themselves in both Italian university life and the unique urban energy of Rome.

As a law exchange student you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad. Generally, the law courses at LUISS have a value of 6 ECTS meaning five (5) courses in total will correspond to 30 ECTS.

The exchange programme is offered for both bachelor and master level law students. Exchange students will be given access to all the courses at Bachelor and/or Master, as long as they have the necessary prerequisites and do not have time-table clashes; 
  The majority of law courses taught in English seem to be offered at master level. The Master courses are at an advanced level and students should have the required background and very good language competences. Courses are taught in either English or Italian.
  The Law programmes at LUISS:
•Master degree program in Law, Digital Innovation and Sustainability
•Single cycle degree program in Giurisprudenza. It is a 5-year law program (which is not divided into a bachelor/master section). However, the first 3 years are equivalent to a BA-program and the last 2 years are equivalent to a master program.

At LUISS some aspects may seem different from what you are used to at the University of Copenhagen; Attendance is mandatory and the relationship between the students and a professor is typically more formal than what you are used to.
The teaching style may contain variations from courses with an active participation requirement (your grade can be partly based on it) to courses with active group work and lectures with primarily one-way communication. At exams there may be many different assessment methods and be aware that many decisions are made solely by the professor in each course - not by the administration.

Course information
Modules/courses on offer to Erasmus law students are from the four years of the LL.B programme, i.e. undergraduate modules. The exchange programme is open for both bachelor and master law students, although only undergraduate courses/modules are available to visiting exchange students.

As a law exchange student you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad and generally the law modules have a value of 5 or 10 ECTS. We recommend that you take third and/or fourth year modules during your exchange programme at Trinity College.
  Scroll down - to see modules/courses available to Law Visiting Students
  •Freshman level refers to first and second year modules – they are core law modules compulsory for all Irish Law students. They are taught in large group settings of up to 200 students.

•Sophister level refers to third and fourth year modules – they are elective law modules, specialising in different field of Law, that are optional for third and fourth year Law students. They can be small classes of 30 students up to 100 students.
  The teaching style at Trinity College is characterized by a great deal of lectures – still with some space for follow-up questions. With course offers exclusively at undergraduate level, the study level especially for master students may seem slightly easier compared to UCPH. As a former law student stated “Trinity had a very good academic programme and I did feel that the level was high at certain times, but I would say that all in all I could relax more during my courses than I would be able to back home.”

Please note that some law modules may not available to exchange students.

UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

Universidade Católica Portuguesa
Description
The Universidade Católica Portuguesa (UCP), established in 1967, is a prominent private university in Portugal with a strong academic and research profile. It operates across four campuses—Lisbon, Porto, Braga, and Viseu—and hosts over 20,000 students, including more than 3,800 international students from 110 nationalities. UCP employs over 1,100 faculty members and maintains 15 research units with nearly 1,500 researchers. The university is known for its interdisciplinary research, international partnerships, and commitment to social responsibility. A distinctive feature of UCP is its status as a concordat university, meaning it operates under a special agreement with the Vatican, which sets it apart from most other institutions in Portugal.
Lisbon, where UCP’s headquarters are located, offers a rich cultural and historical setting for students. The city combines centuries-old architecture with a vibrant modern lifestyle, and is well-connected by public transport, including its iconic yellow trams. Students can enjoy a wide range of cultural activities, from museums and music festivals to traditional Portuguese cuisine. A fun fact: Lisbon’s classic trams are actually based on American streetcars from the early 20th century and were once referred to locally as “Americanos”. This blend of global influences and local traditions makes Lisbon a compelling destination for students seeking both academic and cultural enrichment.

Restrictions
As a law exchange student you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad. Be aware that you will probably not be able to choose sufficient law courses which are taught in English; despite promising information on the homepage of Panthéon-Assas. The courses offered in English are often a mixture of law and social science courses; the latter may not always be pre-approved at UCPH. Therefore, your language level in French must be sufficiently high enabling you to take law courses taught in French.
Course information
You can choose all courses from 1st to 3rd year at the Licence programme equivalent to bachelor level and 4th year courses at Master 1. 
The majority of courses are offered as lectures called ‘cours magistraux’ (CM) and generally they have a duration of three (3) hours. Educational material such as text books are not used at lectures, instead students must take notes from the lecturers' presentations and use these when preparing for the final exams.
In addition to ordinary lectures (CM), you can take two classes called ‘Travaux Dirigés’ (TD). In TD classes education with be interactive with presentations, assignments, group work etc. – an educational style familiar to the teaching methods in Copenhagen. Be aware of a very high workload in these classes. 
  Some courses in law and political science are taught in English. The majority of these courses are taught on 1st or 2nd year at bachelor level. Generally, UCHP recommends that you choose courses preferably from 3rd year law studies and onwards, which require a sufficient language level in French, jf. info at 'Restrictions' above.

  Info on module numbers: The first two characters in a module indicate the subject area of the module e.g. LW1104 where LW indicates it is a law module. The third character indicates the year or level (in this case a First Year or Level One module), and the remaining three characters 112 identify the particular module within the subject area.

The teaching style at University College Cork is characterized by a great deal of lectures and some classroom education. Generally in lectures, there may be little room for discussions or questions. Therefore, if you have any preferences, check out the type of education in advance.
Since Ireland is a common law country, doctrines and principles may be different in specific areas, however, with some similarities to the Danish equivalents. The teaching at UCC may be more focused on learning why the law is as it is, how the law works and critically assessing this. Compared to UCPH with case-based education with focus on learning how to apply the law.

With over 40,000 students, including a large international cohort, the university fosters a vibrant and diverse academic community. UvA is particularly recognized for its research-led teaching, interdisciplinary collaboration, and commitment to addressing global challenges through innovative scholarship.
Located in the heart of Amsterdam, the university offers students the opportunity to study in one of the most culturally rich and globally connected cities in Europe. Amsterdam is known for its historic canals, world-class museums, and dynamic urban life. The city’s compact layout, excellent public transport, and welcoming atmosphere make it ideal for student living. Whether exploring the city’s artistic heritage, engaging in public debates, or enjoying its lively cafés and green spaces, exchange students at UvA will find themselves in an inspiring environment that supports both academic growth and personal discovery.

The Faculty offers three bachelors programmes (two specialised and one general) and approx. 18 masters (LLM) programmes. You may freely choose between LLB courses and LLM courses, but be aware that the majority of courses taught in English are offered at master level.

  Visa requirements:
If you are not a U.S. citizen, a J-1 visa is required for entry to the USA. As an exchange student you must demonstrate access to sufficient funds to cover your living expenses, during the time in the United States. Davis School of Law will provide additional information about this in the admission process.
  Insurance: In addition to a possible mandatory insurance at your host university, UCHP recommends that Danish students on an exchange programme take out an insurance coverage with a Danish insurance company to make sure that you are sufficiently covered especially when talking about possible need for home transportation to DK or possible coverage during your activities and trips outside the campus area of your host university. 
Course information
  Curriculum
The students can choose from the majority of classes in the J.D. curriculum, which corresponds to studies at undergraduate level. Classes are limited in size (especially the seminar classes), so a request does not guarantee registration. The good news is most of our exchange students typically get a good percentage of their top choices
Students must maintain a full course load: 4-5 classes and 10-15 units is a typical course load. When a student submits their class requests, they should identify 15-20 units of classes in total, in case one or two classes are full, or if they decide to drop one class after the first week.
There are certain small number of classes that require prerequisites, but if the exchange student has experience in that area, that can be taken into consideration.

For the fall semester, class schedules will not be released until mid-to-late April. For planning purposes, the majority of the classes that ran this fall will run next fall. However, there will be some changes, as there is a natural effort to expand and update the curriculum and add new, interesting classes for students.
  The style of teaching is based on the socratic method, meaning students are expected to actively participate in class discussions and cold calling may be used.

University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – specially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement.
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

Semester 1: Mid-September to early December. Exams in mid-December.
  Semester 2: Early January to early May. Exams in May.
For more information on the academic calendar click here.
Minimum GPA for admission
The host university requires a GPA minimum of 7.0 (Danish 7-point scale).
Accepted proof of language proficiency (required AFTER nomination)
Glasgow has waived their language test requirements for Danish law exchange students who have a Danish ‘studentereksamen or HF diploma’.

Instead of a TOEFL or IELTS test you apply for a language confirmation from the Faculty of Law in Copenhagen. A language confirmation will only be needed after you have been nominated to the exchange programme in Glasgow.
  The language confirmation will be based on your grades in English from your ‘studentereksamen’ or HF diploma and it will exempt you from taking a TOEFL or IELTS test. You will receive a special language proficiency form from Glasgow after your nomination which you must upload in your application for a language confirmation. 
Study load
60 credits = 30 ECTS
  The normal average workload is 60 Glasgow credits equivalent to 30 ECTS per semester.
   Course levels
Courses at levels 1 and 2 are normally undertaken by Glasgow students during their first two years of study.
Level 3 and 4 courses are upper-level undergraduate courses that require at least two years background experience in the subject area that will be studied at Glasgow.
UCPH encourages our law students to apply for law courses at level 3 or 4. See link to course catalogue below at 'Important links'.
Please note that the majority of courses at level 3 (on third year) and level 4 (on fourth year) are full year courses. For students who arrive in January, courses on level 3 and 4 will be limited.
In connection to full-year courses, semester 1 students can take the first part of the course, for half of the available credits (obviously not an option for semester 2 students).  This means that if you will not be in Glasgow at the time of the examination of the whole year course, you will be asked to submit another piece of assessment in lieu of the examination.

See law course catalogue
The homepage of the Faculty of Law in Glasgow
The Homepage of the University of Glasgow
See info on Student Life
Restrictions
Exchange students can only take undergraduate courses (4-year programme). Postgraduate courses are not accessible. 
Scholarships
Students applying for this university are automatically considered for KU’s Internationalisation scholarship.
Housing
University Accommodation is not guaranteed, but University of Glasgow will guide incoming students in finding accommodation.
Additional information
After you have been nominated by the Faculty of Law in Cph, the University of Glasgow will send you information about their application procedures approximate 3-4 weeks prior to their application deadline.
After the application procedures you will receive information from the University of Glasgow about visa procedures. There may be differences in the requirements for a stay of 6 months' duration compared to a stay beyond that period.
Warning - if you go on student exchange in the last semester of your master degree, a transcript delay may postpone the completion of your master degree! Generally, at British universities official transcripts are delayed with approx. 3-4 months into the next semester due to an audit for a Board of Examiners. In urgent cases, UCPH try to obtain confirmation on the validity of a provisional transcript – but we cannot give you any guarantee in advance.

Restrictions
As a law exchange student, you must follow a full study load of minimum 30 ECTS per semester while you are abroad.
Course information
Exchange programme for both bachelor and master level law students.
  Most courses have the value of 2, 3 or 4 ECTS credits, therefore be aware that you must complete many courses to meet the requirement of 30 ECTS per semester. Incoming Students can choose between undergraduate and graduate courses. See also the information about seminars further down. 

Types of Courses
At the Faculty of Law, different types of courses are offered: In the course catalogue you can see the abbreviations: lectures (VO), courses (KU), practical exercises and case studies (both UE), seminars (SE), repetition courses (RE), conversation classes (KO), Moot Courts (MC), excursions (EX) and practical work courses (PR). When you choose to go to Vienna you will receive additional information about the different categories.

Students can achieve a higher number of ECTS credits in some lectures and seminars. University of Vienna recommends students to follow a mix of courses, lectures and 1-2 seminars.

Seminars: Generally, Vienna recommends that students who enjoy writing papers attend max. two seminars per semester. At first, students will be awarded 4 ECTS if they participate in a seminar and give an oral presentation. Furthermore, you can obtain 4 ECTS additionally, if you write a written paper (approx. 25-page paper) which amounts to 8 ECTS in total per seminar. The course catalogue will only be available a few weeks before the beginning of each semester.
  Vienna generally recommends that international students register for more courses than you need as you may not receive a place in all the courses that you have applied for.

Description
The University of Zurich (UZH), founded in 1833, is the largest university in Switzerland, enrolling around 28,000 students and supported by roughly 3,700 full‑time faculty. It offers the broadest range of subjects of any Swiss higher education institution, with seven faculties covering medicine, law, economics, natural sciences, humanities, theology, and veterinary medicine. UZH is internationally recognized for cutting‑edge research in life sciences, biomedical studies, climate science, and social sciences - featuring leading institutes such as the Institute of Molecular Life Sciences - and encourages a strong interdisciplinary approach. About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

Description
Established in 1477, Uppsala University is the oldest and prestigious university in Sweden and the Nordic region. Located in Uppsala, it offers a wide range of academic disciplines across its nine faculties, including the renowned Faculty of Law. With a strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, the Faculty of Law provides a vibrant research environment and highly regarded legal education. The unique training model and active involvement of prominent researchers ensure high-quality education and enriching research experiences for students. The university's historic legacy and commitment to excellence make it a sought-after destination for students and scholars alike.
With about 52,000 students in a compact city of just over 230,000 residents, Uppsala has a uniquely youthful atmosphere where bikes outnumber cars and student life shapes much of the local culture. The city is known for its beautiful river views, historic university buildings, and cozy cafés that fill with students studying or socializing. A big part of life here revolves around the traditional student “nations”—13 social clubs that organize everything from formal dinners and choirs to club nights and themed parties, making it easy to meet people and dive into Swedish traditions. Despite being a historic city, Uppsala feels dynamic and international, offering plenty of cultural events, outdoor activities, and excellent train connections to Stockholm just 40 minutes away.
