Oslo, the capital of Norway, offers a unique combination of urban life and natural beauty, making it an attractive destination for students. The city is surrounded by forests and fjords, with outdoor activities like hiking, skiing, and kayaking easily accessible via public transport. Oslo is known for its cultural diversity, vibrant arts scene, and commitment to sustainability. Students can enjoy museums, music festivals, and a lively café culture, all within a compact and walkable city center.

Description
The Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU or VU Amsterdam) is a public research university in Amsterdam, Netherlands, founded in 1880. The VU is one of two large, publicly funded research universities in the city, the other being the University of Amsterdam (UvA). The translation of the Dutch name Vrije Universiteit is "Free University". "Free" refers to independence of the university from both the State and the Dutch Reformed Church. Both within and outside the university, the institution is commonly referred to as "the VU".
VU Amsterdam is a unique university with faculties in the humanities, STEM, social sciences and medical sciences. Our education and research are closely linked. We combine our top position in research with a strong social orientation, which has resulted in an excellent international reputation. Our education and research are distinctly multidisciplinary. This is reinforced by the fact that our faculties are all located on one campus. We are a true campus university in the heart of the Zuidas Knowledge District, sharing its excellent national and international position and accessibility. The VU has more than 29.500 students.

Charles University (Prague)
Description
Established in 1348, Charles University stands as one of the oldest and most esteemed universities in the world. Despite its rich historical legacy, the university maintains a forward-thinking and cosmopolitan approach, making it a modern and prestigious institution of higher education. As the largest and top-rated university in the Czech Republic, Charles University boasts 17 faculties, with 14 of them located in the vibrant city of Prague. The university's commitment to internationalization is evident in its diverse student body, with over 9,000 international students comprising approximately 20% of the total student population. This multicultural environment fosters a lively and dynamic atmosphere within the university.
Offering a wide range of courses in multiple languages, including English, German, French, and Czech, Charles University ensures that students from various backgrounds can pursue their academic goals. As an Erasmus student, one can benefit from the support of international associations dedicated to facilitating the integration and orientation process. Additionally, each faculty hosts various clubs and organizations, such as film clubs, choirs, and book clubs, providing students with opportunities to engage in extracurricular activities and explore their diverse interests. Charles University serves as a thriving hub of intellectual growth, cultural exchange, and community engagement, making it a highly sought-after institution for students seeking a comprehensive and enriching educational experience.
Prague offers a rich and stimulating environment for student life, combining historical depth with modern infrastructure. The city is compact and well-connected by public transport, including a metro system known for its depth and efficiency. Students can explore medieval streets, vibrant cultural venues, and green spaces throughout the city. A fun fact: Prague is home to the world’s largest castle complex (the Prague Castle) which spans over 70,000 square meters and includes palaces, churches, and historic lanes, making it a central part of the city’s identity and a frequent destination for students and visitors alike.

Heidelberg University
Description
Established in 1386, the University of Heidelberg holds the distinction of being the oldest university in Germany, embodying a legacy of knowledge and intellectual pursuit that spans centuries. With a community of over 31,000 students, including approximately 6,000 international students, it provides a vibrant and inclusive environment for higher education. Nestled by the picturesque lake Neckar, the campus offers a serene and inspiring backdrop for academic pursuits, fostering a conducive atmosphere for learning and personal growth.
Recognized as one of the strongest research universities in Europe, the institution boasts 12 faculties encompassing a diverse range of disciplines. These faculties, such as Biosciences, Chemistry and Earth Sciences, Mathematics and Computer Science, Physics and Astronomy, Philosophy, Modern Languages, Theology, Law, Economics and Social Sciences, Behavioral and Cultural Studies, and Medicine, offer comprehensive academic programs that cater to a wide array of interests and career paths. Through a commitment to rigorous research and innovation, the university cultivates an environment that fuels intellectual curiosity and advances knowledge in various fields.
Heidelberg is a compact and picturesque city located along the Neckar River in southwestern Germany. Known for its Baroque architecture, cobblestone streets, and vibrant cultural life, the city offers a student-friendly environment with efficient public transport and a strong cycling culture. The city’s historic center is home to cafés, bookstores, and cultural venues, all within walking distance of the university. A fun fact: tucked inside Heidelberg Castle is the world’s largest wine barrel, the Heidelberg Tun, built in 1751 with a capacity of over 219,000 liters—though it was only filled three times due to persistent leaks.

Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

Description
Karolinska Institutet is one of Sweden’s most prominent medical universities, internationally recognized for its research and academic excellence in health sciences. With approximately 6,000 students and over 2,000 doctoral candidates, it maintains a focused academic environment supported by around 5,000 employees, including leading researchers and clinicians. The university is unique in that it is responsible for selecting Nobel laureates in Physiology or Medicine, a distinction that underscores its global influence in biomedical research. Its close integration with Stockholm’s healthcare system provides students with direct access to clinical environments and research facilities.
Located in the Stockholm region, Karolinska Institutet offers students the opportunity to live and study in one of Europe’s most technologically advanced and environmentally conscious capitals. The city combines efficient public transport, widespread English proficiency, and a strong cultural infrastructure, making it accessible and navigable for international students. Stockholm is built on 14 islands connected by over 50 bridges, and its proximity to forests and waterways allows for outdoor activities year-round. A small curiosity: the city’s metro system is often referred to as the world’s longest art gallery, with installations and sculptures integrated into nearly every station.

Firmly rooted in the heart of Paris, its historical roots trace back to 1257, making it one of the oldest universities in Europe. With its three faculties encompassing Arts & Humanities, Medicine, and Science and Engineering, the university is dedicated to nurturing student excellence and addressing the scientific complexities of the modern era. Serving as a beacon of higher education and research, Sorbonne University holds a distinguished position within the French academic landscape.
Furthermore, Sorbonne is a part of 4EU+ Alliance with the purpose of laying the foundation for an innovative European university model. Sorbonne is fully committed to engage with both its students and partners on an international scale. The university believes that internalization and innovation go hand in hand. In terms of student life, Sorbonne offers more than 80 sporting and artistic activities which are free to join as students off the university.

The clinical rotations at Sorbonne University last one month. This means that you must participate in three rotations during your exchange.

ECTS credits for clinical rotations:
Each clinical rotation (each lasting one month) equates to 3 ECTS credits. In order to obtain advance approval of the transfer of credits, you must do a minimum of 12 weeks of clinical training.

Description
Tokai University is a private university established by Dr. Shigeyoshi Matsumae in1942. "Tokai" of "Tokai University" refers to the sea to the east of Asia, namely the Pacific Ocean, and symbolizes the desire to foster a rich spirit and broad perspectives. Tokai University consists of 7 campuses in Sapporo, Takanawa, Yoyogi, Shonan, Isehara, Shimizu and Kumamoto, which are comprised of 19 schools and colleges made up of 75 departments, majors, and programs. Its graduate schools offer 17 programs. The total student population is approximately 30,000. The founding spirit and objectives of Tokai University are to contribute to human society, to foster active, talented people and to arouse interest in our earth and a sense of responsibility for the future of mankind. Tokai University's educational guidelines ground these objectives by offering a sound and reliable way of thinking as a basis for living and cultivating the richness of humanity. Tokai University maintains academic exchange agreements with more than 120 universities/organizations in approx. 40 countries. A wide range of international exchange activities are being actively pursued, from those on the academic front involving students, researchers, joint-research projects, and the exchange of academic resources, to cultural and athletic exchanges. There are about 960 international students on campus. Tokai University has five offices abroad: Denmark, Austria, USA (junior college in Hawaii), Korea and Thailand. Description
UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

Description
The University of Crete, established in 1973, is a public, research-oriented institution with campuses in Rethymno and Heraklion. It serves a student body of approximately 20,000 undergraduates and 2,500 postgraduates, supported by over 500 academic staff and 420 administrative personnel. The university is organized into five schools and sixteen departments, offering a wide range of academic disciplines. It is known for its strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, participating actively in European programs such as Erasmus and maintaining partnerships with institutions across Europe and the United States. A unique feature of the University of Crete is its close integration with regional research centers, including the Foundation for Research and Technology – Hellas (FORTH), which enhances opportunities for students to engage in high-level scientific work.
Crete, the largest and most populous of the Greek islands, offers a rich cultural and natural environment for students. The island combines ancient history, Mediterranean landscapes, and a vibrant local culture shaped by diverse influences from Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Students can explore archaeological sites, hike through dramatic gorges, and enjoy local cuisine rooted in the renowned Cretan diet. Public transport connects the university campuses with nearby towns and beaches, making it easy to experience the island’s varied offerings. A fun fact: Crete is home to two of the world’s few pink sand beaches, Balos and Elafonissi, adding a unique natural attraction to the student experience.

Description
Situated between the Alps and the Jura mountains, at the southwestern tip of Switzerland and Lake Geneva, Geneva is in the heart of Western Europe. Its ideal geographical location has made the city a prosperous hub for trade and exchanges of all kinds. This cosmopolitan city is home to more than 180 nationalities and is recognized worldwide for its spirit of openness and tolerance.
Considered the smallest of the major world capitals, Geneva, which is home to some thirty international organizations including the UN, WTO, WHO and ILO and more than 300 NGOs and permanent missions of foreign states, has a true international vocation.This multicultural environment is reflected at the University of Geneva, where about one third of the students are foreign nationals. The university pursues a very active policy of international relations; in particular, it participates in numerous bi- or multilateral agreements, networks and programs such as Erasmus and Socrates.
Ideally located near the most famous ski areas of the Alps, not far from the Mediterranean, while being anchored in Central Europe, Geneva offers the rare advantages of a cosmopolitan city on a human scale. Cuisine and music from all over the world are found here. Lovers of green spaces will find an atmosphere conducive to meditation in its parks or on the shores of Lake Geneva. Culture lovers will be spoilt for choice, thanks to the presence in Geneva of one of the most prestigious opera scenes in Europe, several film festivals, and numerous concert halls and theaters.
The University of Geneva takes an active part in this cultural scene. Its film club and choir in particular attract large audiences. But Geneva also lives to the rhythm of a multitude of associations, representing the most diverse countries and interests, which make it uniquely suited for the exchange of ideas and traditions.

Description
The University of Zurich (UZH), founded in 1833, is the largest university in Switzerland, enrolling around 28,000 students and supported by roughly 3,700 full‑time faculty. It offers the broadest range of subjects of any Swiss higher education institution, with seven faculties covering medicine, law, economics, natural sciences, humanities, theology, and veterinary medicine. UZH is internationally recognized for cutting‑edge research in life sciences, biomedical studies, climate science, and social sciences - featuring leading institutes such as the Institute of Molecular Life Sciences - and encourages a strong interdisciplinary approach. About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

Description
Established in 1477, Uppsala University is the oldest and prestigious university in Sweden and the Nordic region. Located in Uppsala, it offers a wide range of academic disciplines across its nine faculties, including the renowned Faculty of Law. With a strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, the Faculty of Law provides a vibrant research environment and highly regarded legal education. The unique training model and active involvement of prominent researchers ensure high-quality education and enriching research experiences for students. The university's historic legacy and commitment to excellence make it a sought-after destination for students and scholars alike.
With about 52,000 students in a compact city of just over 230,000 residents, Uppsala has a uniquely youthful atmosphere where bikes outnumber cars and student life shapes much of the local culture. The city is known for its beautiful river views, historic university buildings, and cozy cafés that fill with students studying or socializing. A big part of life here revolves around the traditional student “nations”—13 social clubs that organize everything from formal dinners and choirs to club nights and themed parties, making it easy to meet people and dive into Swedish traditions. Despite being a historic city, Uppsala feels dynamic and international, offering plenty of cultural events, outdoor activities, and excellent train connections to Stockholm just 40 minutes away.

Description
Founded in 1948, Sciences Po Lyon is a public higher education institute specializing in Human and Social Sciences and training future executives in the public and private sectors. The educational model is similar to that of Anglo-Saxon Schools of Public Affairs or School of Government. Their teaching is fueled by a constant quest for excellence and cultivates a multi-disciplinary approach in human and social sciences based on four fundamental disciplines: history, law, political science, and economy.
Since 2017, Sciences Po Lyon has operated two campuses: the historical campus in Lyon city centre and a second in Saint-Étienne. International students who visit us for a semester or a full academic year will study on the Lyon campus.
With its 1,800 students, Sciences Po Lyon is a higher education institute of a size that enables it to retain a human touch, offering optimal study conditions that can benefit each and every individual student.

Description
Institut d'Études Politiques de Toulouse, commonly known as Sciences Po Toulouse, is a selective and prestigious institution specializing in political science, international relations, law, economics, and sociology. Founded in 1948, it is part of the network of Sciences Po institutes across France and is recognized for its rigorous academic programs and strong emphasis on interdisciplinary research. Sciences Po Toulouse offers a dynamic learning environment with a student body of around 1,500, including a growing number of international students. The institution is particularly committed to preparing students for careers in public service, international organizations, and policy-making, while also fostering critical thinking and civic engagement.
Located in the vibrant city of Toulouse, Sciences Po offers students a unique blend of academic excellence and quality of life. Toulouse is the fourth-largest city in France and a major center for aerospace, innovation, and higher education. Known for its rich history, lively cultural scene, and welcoming atmosphere, the city provides an ideal setting for student life. With its mix of historic architecture, green spaces, and bustling cafés, Toulouse offers both inspiration and comfort. Exchange students at Sciences Po Toulouse benefit not only from a stimulating academic experience but also from the opportunity to explore a dynamic and youthful city in the heart of southern France.

The university fosters a vibrant student community through various academic and cultural events, allowing exchange students to immerse themselves in both Italian university life and the unique urban energy of Rome.

UiT The Arctic University of Norway
Description
UiT is a large, multi-campus university in Northern Norway, with around 17,000 students, globally recognised as a leading centre for Arctic research. It offers a broad spectrum of programmes across its eight faculties covering most disciplines. While Norwegian is the main language of instruction, many courses especially at the master’s level are taught in English. 
The city of Tromsø is often described as really special: you’re surrounded by dramatic Arctic scenery, with fjords, mountains, and islands all nearby, and you get phenomena like the Midnight Sun in summer and Northern Lights in winter. Tromsø is reasonably walkable, especially in the city centre with decent public transit. Importantly, the setting encourages a lot of outdoor activity, both in winter and summer, so if you enjoy nature, you’ll have plenty of options for hiking, skiing, boating, and exploring both urban and wild landscapes.

Description
The Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB), established in 1968, is a leading public university in Spain known for its strong research orientation and international outlook. It serves a student population of over 35,000 and offers a wide range of academic disciplines across its faculties. UAB is recognized for its interdisciplinary approach, innovative teaching methods, and close collaboration with research institutes and industry partners. The university’s main campus is designed as a self-contained academic community, providing students with access to modern facilities, libraries, and support services in a single location.
Located just outside Barcelona, UAB offers students the advantages of a campus environment while being close to one of Europe’s most dynamic cities. Barcelona is known for its rich cultural life, Mediterranean climate, and excellent public transport, making it easy for students to commute between the campus and the city. The region offers a mix of historic sites, beaches, and vibrant neighborhoods, providing a diverse and stimulating setting for student life.

Description
Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne is one of France’s leading public universities, with a strong academic tradition rooted in the historic Sorbonne. It serves over 40,000 students and employs more than 1,400 academic and administrative staff. The university is known for its focus on humanities, social sciences, law, and economics, and maintains active research centres across these fields. It plays a central role in French academic life and contributes to international research networks. Its structure supports a broad academic offering while maintaining close links to national institutions and cultural organisations, making it a distinctive institution within the French higher education system.
The university is located in central Paris, with sites spread across several historic buildings in the Latin Quarter and other parts of the city. This location places students in direct contact with the intellectual and cultural life of the capital, with access to libraries, archives, museums, and public institutions. Paris offers a wide range of academic and cultural resources, as well as efficient transport connections to other regions and countries. For students considering an exchange semester, Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne provides an opportunity to study in a city closely tied to European academic and cultural history.

About the agreement between the University of Copenhagen (UCPH) and the University of Edinburgh (UoE): 
1 university-wide agreement, covering subject areas within Arts, Humanities, Social Sciences and Science, plus:
University of Edinburgh – university-wide
6 subject specific agreements in the following high-pressure subject areas at UoE: Law, English, Sociology, Anthropology, Psychology, and Political Science.

                        (Please note that there is a seperate fact sheet for each agreement)
University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – specially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement. 
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

A defining feature of Freiburg is its close collaboration with external research institutions such as the Fraunhofer and Max Planck Societies, offering students access to a broad academic network and research opportunities beyond the university itself.
Situated in the southwest of Germany near the borders of France and Switzerland, Freiburg is a vibrant university town known for its compact layout, green surroundings, and active student life. The city has a population of about 230,000 and is often cited as one of Germany’s most environmentally conscious and bicycle-friendly cities. Freiburg’s location at the edge of the Black Forest provides easy access to hiking, skiing, and other outdoor activities, making it an attractive destination for students seeking both academic engagement and recreational opportunities. Its international atmosphere and strong local support for education contribute to a welcoming environment for exchange students.

The University of Göttingen, officially Georg-August-Universität Göttingen, is a research-oriented public university with a strong international reputation, particularly in the natural sciences and humanities. Göttingen’s location offers easy access to other major cities such as Hanover and Kassel, while its green surroundings and historical architecture provide a pleasant environment for study and daily life. The University’s twenty sites are located in and around Salzburg’s historical center, which is a UNESCO listed world heritage site. Situated in the heart of Europe, Salzburg offers fantastic connections to neighbouring countries and to some of Austria’s most beautiful alpine national parks. It is also a culturally vibrant city, home to world-famous performing arts and music festivals. Operating in this setting, the University of Salzburg offers international students and scholars abroad a unique range of experiences.

Description
The University of Zurich (UZH), founded in 1833, is the largest university in Switzerland, enrolling around 28,000 students and supported by roughly 3,700 full‑time faculty. It offers the broadest range of subjects of any Swiss higher education institution, with seven faculties covering medicine, law, economics, natural sciences, humanities, theology, and veterinary medicine. UZH is internationally recognized for cutting‑edge research in life sciences, biomedical studies, climate science, and social sciences - featuring leading institutes such as the Institute of Molecular Life Sciences - and encourages a strong interdisciplinary approach. About 25% of students come from abroad, creating a vibrant multicultural academic environment with many research partnerships and study programs open to international applicants.
Living in Zurich as a student means immersing yourself in a cosmopolitan yet manageable city with excellent transport and abundant social opportunities. Zurich’s well‑connected public transport and bike‑friendly streets make getting around easy, with student discounts available. Campus life is dynamic - UZH’s Academic Sports Association (ASVZ) offers over 120 sports activities, while the student union (VSUZH) and a dozen-plus associations organize academic, cultural, and political events. International students can join clubs ranging from acapella ensembles and environmental groups to investment clubs and political societies. Although Zurich’s cost of living is high, many students find shared flats through WOKO or nearby towns, and experience suggests that engagement in sports and social events helps with integration, even for those new to German. Overall, UZH offers a rich blend of top-tier academics, diverse extracurriculars, and Swiss urban life steeped in culture and connection.

Established in 1477, Uppsala University is the oldest and prestigious university in Sweden and the Nordic region. Located in Uppsala, it offers a wide range of academic disciplines across its nine faculties, including the renowned Faculty of Law. With a strong emphasis on research and international collaboration, the Faculty of Law provides a vibrant research environment and highly regarded legal education. The unique training model and active involvement of prominent researchers ensure high-quality education and enriching research experiences for students. The university's historic legacy and commitment to excellence make it a sought-after destination for students and scholars alike.
With about 52,000 students in a compact city of just over 230,000 residents, Uppsala has a uniquely youthful atmosphere where bikes outnumber cars and student life shapes much of the local culture. The city is known for its beautiful river views, historic university buildings, and cozy cafés that fill with students studying or socializing. A big part of life here revolves around the traditional student “nations”—13 social clubs that organize everything from formal dinners and choirs to club nights and themed parties, making it easy to meet people and dive into Swedish traditions. Despite being a historic city, Uppsala feels dynamic and international, offering plenty of cultural events, outdoor activities, and excellent train connections to Stockholm just 40 minutes away.

Description
The Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU or VU Amsterdam) is a public research university in Amsterdam, Netherlands, founded in 1880. The VU is one of two large, publicly funded research universities in the city, the other being the University of Amsterdam (UvA). The translation of the Dutch name Vrije Universiteit is "Free University". "Free" refers to independence of the university from both the State and the Dutch Reformed Church. Both within and outside the university, the institution is commonly referred to as "the VU".
VU Amsterdam is a unique university with faculties in the humanities, STEM, social sciences and medical sciences. Our education and research are closely linked. We combine our top position in research with a strong social orientation, which has resulted in an excellent international reputation. Our education and research are distinctly multidisciplinary. This is reinforced by the fact that our faculties are all located on one campus. We are a true campus university in the heart of the Zuidas Knowledge District, sharing its excellent national and international position and accessibility. The VU has more than 29.500 students.

Description
Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. E. B. Du Bois, Angela Davis, Arthur Schopenhauer, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, Walter Benjamin, Max Weber, Georg Simmel, Karl Liebknecht, Ernst Cassirer, Heinrich Heine, Max Planck, and the Brothers Grimm.

Alternatively, students can do an online language placement test (C-Test) offered by the Language Centre at Humboldt Universität.
  Generally, applicants (non-native speakers) who plan to participate in English speaking courses only, are required to prove English language proficiency at level B2 or higher (equivalent TOEFL: min. 80, IELTS: min. 6.0.). Restrictions
Special language and math requirements for the School of Business and Economics
The language of instruction of all the department's Master programs is English. The courses at the School of Business and Economics have a strong emphasis on imparting rigorous and quantitative methods of economic analysis and management science. A strong background in Mathematics is essential.
  Access to admission restricted undergraduate courses:
Applicants from EU-Countries as well as from Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway are allowed to apply for a maximum of three admission-restricted (NC) undergraduate courses (Bachelor’s or State Examination courses). Furthermore, these applicants are allowed to indicate (and apply for) an additional non-admission-restricted (non-NC) course of studies in the application.
  Access to admission restricted Master courses
Applicants (whether EU or non-EU applicants) to Master’s courses are allowed to apply for only one admission-restricted (NC) Master’s course and additionally for one non-admission-restricted (non-NC) degree course.

Description
The University of Amsterdam (UvA) is one of Europe’s top research universities, known for its academic excellence, broad disciplinary offerings, and strong international orientation. Founded in 1632, UvA offers a wide range of programs across seven faculties, including humanities, social and behavioral sciences, economics and business, law, and science. With over 40,000 students, including a large international cohort, the university fosters a vibrant and diverse academic community. UvA is particularly recognized for its research-led teaching, interdisciplinary collaboration, and commitment to addressing global challenges through innovative scholarship.
Located in the heart of Amsterdam, the university offers students the opportunity to study in one of the most culturally rich and globally connected cities in Europe. Amsterdam is known for its historic canals, world-class museums, and dynamic urban life. The city’s compact layout, excellent public transport, and welcoming atmosphere make it ideal for student living. Whether exploring the city’s artistic heritage, engaging in public debates, or enjoying its lively cafés and green spaces, exchange students at UvA will find themselves in an inspiring environment that supports both academic growth and personal discovery.

University-wide agreement:
Students applying through the university-wide agreement are not guaranteed full course enrolment in high pressure subject areas – specially at honours level. On the other hand, they would be allowed to choose courses across different subject areas. This agreement is for students with a more flexible ‘mobility window’, and for students prepared to take all or some courses outside the high-pressure subject areas.
  Subject specific agreements:
The 6 subject specific agreements cover almost all high-pressure subject areas at UoE and are only for students enrolled in the corresponding study programme at UCPH: Law, English/English Literature, Anthropology, Sociology and Political Science.
Students applying through a subject specific agreement must take minimum 2/3 of the courses from the subject area of their nomination/subject specific agreement.
Please note that although History and Art History are considered high pressure subject areas at UoE, we do not have subject specific agreements in these study areas, as restrictions on course access for History/Art History has not been an issue for UCPH students so far.

Description
The University of Trento (Università degli Studi di Trento) is located in Trento and nearby Rovereto. It has been able to achieve considerable results in didactics, research, and international relations according to CENSIS and the Italian Ministry of Education. The University of Trento was founded in 1962 as a Higher University Institute for Social Sciences. It then became the first Faculty of Sociology in Italy. UniTrento is widely recognized as a dynamic and innovative research and teaching institution with strong connections to industry and top-class services. National and international classifications consistently rank the University of Trento as one of the top Italian universities. In addition to traditional lectures, there is much emphasis on laboratory work and small seminar groups.

UniTrento is a medium-sized university, where it is easy for students and teachers to interact and there is a continuous exchange of ideas across many different disciplines: in short, the virtuous dynamism of a small university. Furthermore, because of long-term international relations, UniTrento is a vibrant, multi-cultural university which fosters an enriching and stimulating learning environment. The university is located in one of the most gorgeous natural environments in the world, the Dolomites, and it combines strong links to the territory with a steady expansion of international outreach.

Description
Founded in 1365, the University of Vienna is one of Europe’s oldest and most prestigious institutions of higher learning. Today, it hosts approximately 85,000 students, including nearly 30,000 international students from over 120 countries. With 15 faculties and 5 centres, the university offers an exceptionally broad academic portfolio, encompassing 187 degree programmes across disciplines such as humanities, natural sciences, social sciences, law, and economics. Renowned for its cutting-edge research, the university has received over 140 ERC grants and proudly counts Nobel laureates among its faculty. Exchange students benefit from a wide selection of English-taught courses, modern facilities, and a vibrant academic community committed to innovation and global engagement.
Located in the heart of Austria’s capital, the University of Vienna offers students the opportunity to live and study in one of the world’s most liveable cities. Vienna is a cultural and intellectual hub, known for its rich history, classical music heritage, and dynamic arts scene. With over 2 million residents and a strong international presence, the city combines historical charm with modern infrastructure, excellent public transport, and a high quality of life. Students can enjoy everything from world-class museums and concert halls to green parks and cafés, all within walking distance of the university’s central campus. For those seeking both academic enrichment and cultural exploration, Vienna provides an ideal setting for a memorable and rewarding exchange experience.

Description
The Universidad Complutense de Madrid (UCM), founded in 1293, is one of the oldest and most prestigious universities in Spain and Europe. With over 86,000 students and a wide range of academic disciplines, it is also one of the largest traditional (non-distance) universities in Europe. UCM is a public research university with a strong emphasis on academic excellence, social responsibility, and international collaboration. Its two main campuses—Moncloa and Somosaguas—are located in Madrid and offer students access to extensive libraries, research centers, and cultural facilities. The university has a long history of academic innovation and was among the first in the world to award doctoral degrees to women.
Madrid, the capital of Spain, offers a dynamic and student-friendly environment with a rich cultural heritage and excellent infrastructure. The city’s extensive public transport system—including metro, buses, and commuter trains—makes it easy for students to navigate between campuses and explore the city. Madrid is known for its world-class museums, lively neighborhoods, and green spaces like Retiro Park. A fun fact: Madrid is one of the highest capital cities in Europe, sitting at 667 meters above sea level, and its name is believed to come from the Arabic word Magerit, meaning “place of abundant water.” This blend of history, accessibility, and vibrant city life makes it an ideal destination for students.

Description
The Universidad de Extremadura (UEx), founded in 1973, is a public university serving the autonomous community of Extremadura in western Spain. It has a student population of over 20,000 and operates across four campuses located in Badajoz, Cáceres, Mérida, and Plasencia. UEx offers a wide range of academic disciplines and is known for its commitment to regional development through education, research, and innovation. The university maintains strong ties with local institutions and industries, and its facilities include libraries, laboratories, research centers, and sports complexes. Its structure and mission reflect a focus on accessibility, community engagement, and practical learning.
Extremadura offers a distinctive setting for student life, with a mix of historical towns, natural landscapes, and cultural traditions. The region is less crowded than other parts of Spain, making it ideal for students seeking a quieter and more immersive experience. Public transport connects the university campuses with surrounding cities and countryside, and students can explore UNESCO World Heritage sites, Roman ruins, and medieval architecture. A fun fact: Extremadura is the birthplace of many Spanish conquistadors, and cities like Medellín and Trujillo in Latin America are named after towns in the region. This historical legacy adds a unique dimension to studying in Extremadura.

Description
The University of Göttingen, officially Georg-August-Universität Göttingen, is a research-oriented public university with a strong international reputation, particularly in the natural sciences and humanities. Founded in 1737, it currently enrolls around 30,000 students and employs over 4,000 academic staff. The university is part of several major research networks and maintains close ties with institutions such as the Max Planck Society, which has multiple institutes located in Göttingen. A notable feature of the university is its emphasis on academic freedom and its long-standing tradition of fostering critical thinking and interdisciplinary collaboration.
Göttingen seems to pulsate with a thirst for knowledge. It is a city shaped by students and academic life more than anywhere else in Germany. A total of 44 Nobel laureates are associated with Göttingen. Some were born in the city, while others came here to study, teach or conduct research. Göttingen is a compact university town in central Germany, with a population of approximately 120,000. Known as the "City of Knowledge", the city’s identity is closely tied to its academic community, and students make up a significant portion of the population. This creates a lively and student-focused atmosphere, with a wide range of cultural events, cafés, and public spaces geared toward young people. Göttingen’s location offers easy access to other major cities such as Hanover and Kassel, while its green surroundings and historical architecture provide a pleasant environment for study and daily life. The city’s manageable size and strong support for student initiatives make it particularly welcoming for exchange students.

Humboldt University of Berlin is a public research university in the central borough of Mitte in Berlin, Germany. It was established by Frederick William III on the initiative of Wilhelm von Humboldt, Johann Gottlieb Fichte and Friedrich Ernst Daniel Schleiermacher as the University of Berlin (Universität zu Berlin) in 1809, and opened in 1810, making it the oldest of Berlin's four universities.
HU is divided into nine faculties, including its medical school shared with the Free University of Berlin, has a student enrollment of around 33,000 students, and offers degree programmes in some 189 disciplines from undergraduate to postdoctorate level.
Past and present faculty and notable alumni include an impressive 57 Nobel Prize laureates as well as eminent philosophers, sociologists, artists, lawyers, politicians, mathematicians, scientists, and Heads of State; among them are Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Friedrich Engels, Otto von Bismarck, W. Accepted proof of language proficiency (required AFTER nomination)
Be aware that 95% of all courses/modules are taught in German. 
The required level of German language skills is B2 on the European CEFR- self-assessment grid - and can be proven by submitting a Goethe-Zertifikat B2/C1, DSH or TestDaF.
Alternatively, all incoming exchange students take an online German language test (called the C-Test). After the application round in Cph there will be an application process for Humboldt where this test can be taken online with texts to be filled in and parts of reading, understanding, and giving summaries; the C-test is all in writing, no oral part.

If your result is less than 50 points, you must apply for an intensive course in German at the Humboldt Universität (HU) language center (Sprachenzentrum) and send the confirmation of this application to HU. Preconditions for the admission will be the passed test (equivalent to level B2 with 50 points) or the intensive course.
The language centre of Humboldt Universität offers a number of language courses for international students as well as intensive courses before the beginning of each semester (or during the semester).

Description
Trinity College Dublin, founded in 1592 by royal charter, is Ireland’s oldest and most prestigious university and the sole college of the University of Dublin. Located on a 47-acre historic campus in the heart of Dublin, it serves around 22,000 undergraduate and postgraduate students across three faculties: Arts, Humanities and Social Sciences; Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics; and Health Sciences. Trinity is internationally recognized for academic excellence and offers a broad range of programs across disciplines such as business, law, engineering, science, and the arts. The university fosters independent intellectual inquiry, interdisciplinary collaboration, and creativity, and is consistently ranked as Ireland’s leading university and among the top 100 globally in various fields.
Trinity is committed to research excellence and societal impact, with world-class interdisciplinary research centres focused on areas like sustainable development, global health, artificial intelligence, and biodiversity. The university hosts a diverse and international academic community, with students and staff from over 120 countries. Trinity encourages a broad-based education, allowing students to explore subjects outside their primary field of study and develop critical thinking skills. With strong links to global industries and institutions, and a location in Dublin’s innovation and cultural hub, Trinity offers an education that is both academically rigorous and closely connected to real-world opportunities.

Description
The University of Bern, founded in 1834, is one of Switzerland’s leading comprehensive universities, enrolling around 19,000 students across eight faculties and offering a wide spectrum of programs in the humanities, natural sciences, law, economics, medicine, and social sciences. With a strong focus on interdisciplinary research, sustainability, and global cooperation, the university ranks among the top 120–200 universities worldwide, depending on the field. It is particularly renowned for cutting-edge research in climate science, space, and health. Students benefit from close links to governmental institutions and international organizations located in Bern, providing unique opportunities for internships, networking, and real-world projects.
Living in Bern as a student means enjoying all the advantages of Switzerland’s picturesque capital city. With its UNESCO-listed medieval Old Town, lively café culture, and riverside spots along the Aare for swimming or relaxing, Bern combines a laid-back atmosphere with rich cultural offerings. The university’s facilities are mostly concentrated in the welcoming Länggasse district, where student life thrives thanks to numerous associations, events, and support services like the Welcome Center and student union. Excellent public transport, bike-friendly streets, and proximity to the Alps make it easy to explore both the city and Switzerland’s stunning natural landscapes, offering an outstanding quality of life alongside your studies.

The University of Sydney requires that veterinary students are vaccinated against Q fever before they can participate in clinical training. You must therefore arrive in Australia two weeks before the teaching starts, so you can be vaccinated. The vaccine costs AUD 255 (approx. DKK 1,300).

In principle, the University of Sydney offers accommodation for international students. You apply for accommodation after you have applied for admission as an exchange student.

Study plan
TiHo offers clinical rotations at their six clinics, reproduction and emergency room:

• Clinic for Poultry
• Clinic for Small Mammals, Reptiles and Birds
• Small Animals Clinic
• Clinic for Swine, Small Ruminants and Forensic Medicine
• Clinic for Horses
• Clinic for Cattle
• Unit of Reproductive Medicine of the clinics
• Emergency Service

Each clinic week equates to 1.5 ECTS credits.

The number of weeks you can spend in the different clinics depends on the available capacity.

If you are going to do an exchange at TiHo, you need to plan your studies together with the TiHo coordinator.

Study plan
Vienna offers various clinical courses for exchange students.

You must attend the courses for a minimum of four weeks. You will put together your study plan with the coordinator in Vienna.

The following clinical courses are offered: University Clinic for Swine:
• Swine Medicine

University Clinic for Poultry and Fish:
• Poultry Medicine
• Fish Medicine

University Clinic for Ruminants:
• Ruminant Medicine

University Clinic for Small Animals:
• Anaesthesiology and Perioperative Intensive Care (PICU)
• Diagnostic Imaging
• Obstetrics, Gynaecology and Andrology
• Internal Medicine and Small Animals
• Small Animal Surgery
• Pet Birds and Reptile Medicine

University Clinic for Horses:
• Equine Internal Medicine
• Equine Surgery

Insemination and Embryotransfer Platform

Study plan
Utrecht offers 10 weeks of clinical training and/or the opportunity to do a project in practice (research project) with a research group.

Clinical rotations You will complete the clinical rotations at the three hospitals:
• The Companion Animals Clinic
• The Equine Clinic
• The Farm Animals Clinic

Each rotation lasts five weeks, which means that you will be able to do two rotations during your exchange.

You can participate in clinical rotations at all three hospitals or only at one hospital.

A normal day in the clinic starts at 8 am and ends at 5 pm. However, this may vary depending on the number of patients and cases.

Each clinic week equates to 1.5 ECTS credits. Doing a 10-week clinic equates to 15 ECTS credits.

Project in practice/research project
If you want to do a project in practice during your stay at Utrecht University, you must mention this when applying for admission once you have been recommended for an exchange place.

You will then make an agreement directly with the research group with the help of the coordinator in Utrecht.

Read more about the project in practice on Study Information at UCPH.
